revision.comment: initial text of stub article -- 1st steps in a constructive direction
revision.text:
{{nihongo|'''Takebe Shrine'''|建部大社|Takebe-taisha}} is a [[Shinto]] shrine located at [[Ōtsu, Shiga|Ōtsu]] in [[Shiga Prefecture]], [[Japan]]. The shrine is also known in Japanese (esp. formerly) as {{nihongo3||建部神社|Takebe-jinja}}. It was the ''ichinomiya of [[Ōmi province]].<ref>Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1964). ''Visiting Famous Shrines in Japan,'' pp. 308-323.</ref>
The Shrine was established in the 43rd year of the reign of [[Emperor Keiko]].<ref>Ponsonby-Fane, Richarc. 1964). ''Visiting Shrines,'' p. 313.</ref>
The Shrine became the object of Imperial patronage during the early [[Heian period]] and it was accorded some attention during the [[Kamakura period]]; however, the Shrine was amongst the many which failed to prosper during the difficult years of the [[Sengoku period]].<ref>Ponsonby-Fane, ''Visiting Shrines,'' pp. 320-321.</ref>
* '''1868''' (''Meiji 1, 9th month''): When Emperor Meiji mad his first visit to [[Edo]], he dispatched an emmisary to the shrine with a''heikaku'' for the kami.<ref>Ponsonby-Farne, ''Visiting Shrines,'' p. 321.</ref>
From 1871 through 1946, the Tatsuta Shrine was officially designated one of the ''Kanpei-taisha'' (官幣大社), meaning that it stood in the first rank of government supported shrines. <ref>Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 124.</ref>
== See also== * [[List of Shinto shrines]] * [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]] * [[Ōmi Maru]]
==Notes== {{reflist|2}}
==References== {{refbegin}} * [[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane]], Richard Arthur Brabazon. (1962). [http://books.google.com/books?id=tjEvAAAAYAAJ&dq=Studies+in+Shinto+and+Shrines&ei=O6yMSZu3HKD2MsWNhdwC&client=firefox-a ''Studies in Shinto and Shrines.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/3994492 OCLC 399449] * ____________. (1959). [http://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&q=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan&dq=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan&client=firefox-a&pgis=1 ''The Imperial House of Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/194887 OCLC 194887] * ____________. (1964). [http://books.google.com/books?id=_6rXAAAAMAAJ&q=Visiting+famous+shrines+in+japan&dq=Visiting+famous+shrines+in+japan&client=firefox-a ''Visiting Famous Shrines in Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby-Fane Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/oclc/1030156 OCLC 1030156] {{refend}}
{{DEFAULTSORT:Takebe Shrine}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Nara Prefecture]]
{{Shinto-stub}}
[[ja:建部大社]]

{{Infobox Shinto shrine | name = Takebe taisha
建部大社 | image = Takebe Taisha2.jpg | caption = Takebe taisha | type = | dedication = | founded = | closed = | founder = | priest = | address = | phone = | website = }} {{nihongo|'''Takebe taisha'''|建部大社|}} is a [[Shinto]] shrine located at [[Ōtsu, Shiga|Ōtsu]] in [[Shiga Prefecture]], [[Japan]]. The shrine is also known in Japanese (esp. formerly) as {{nihongo3||建部神社|Takebe-jinja}}.Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1964). ''Visiting Famous Shrines in Japan,'' pp. 308–323. ==History== The shrine was established in the 43rd year of the reign of [[Emperor Keiko]].Ponsonby-Fane, Richarc. 1964). ''Visiting Shrines,'' p. 313. The shrine became the object of Imperial patronage during the early [[Heian period]] and it was accorded some attention during the [[Kamakura period]]; however, the Shrine was amongst the many which failed to prosper during the difficult years of the [[Sengoku period]].Ponsonby-Fane, ''Visiting Shrines,'' pp. 320-321. * '''1868''' (''Meiji 1, 9th month''): When Emperor Meiji mad his first visit to [[Edo]], he dispatched an emissary to the shrine with a''heikaku'' for the kami.Ponsonby-Farne, ''Visiting Shrines,'' p. 321. Takebe was designated as the chief Shinto shrine (''[[ichinomiya]]'') for the former [[Ōmi province]]. [http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/images/uploads/EOS070712Ab.pdf "Nationwide List of ''Ichinomiya''," p. 1.]; retrieved 2011-08-09 From 1871 through 1946, the Takebe was officially designated one of the {{nihongo|''[[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines|Kanpei-taisha]]''|官幣大社}},Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 124. meaning that it stood in the first rank of government supported shrines which were especially venerated by the imperial family. ==Festivals== Annual festivals at Takebe include modern events like the Reisai in mid-AprilPonsonby-Farne, ''Visiting Shrines,'' p. 322. and older rites such as * ''Gokokusai'' or ''Gokoku no matsuri'' in early February.Ponsonby-Farne, ''Visiting Shrines,'' pp. 322-323. * ''Funayiki no matsuri'' in mid AugustPonsonby-Farne, ''Visiting Shrines,'' p. 323. * ''Chinkwa-sai'' in late November.Ponsonby-Farne, ''Visiting Shrines,'' pp. 323-324. == See also== * [[List of Shinto shrines]] * [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]] * [[Ōmi Maru]] ==Notes== {{reflist|2}} ==References== {{refbegin}} * [[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane], Richard]]. (1959). [http://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&q=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan&dq=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan&client=firefox-a&pgis=1 ''The Imperial House of Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/194887 OCLC 194887] * ____________. (1964). [http://books.google.com/books?id=_6rXAAAAMAAJ&q=Visiting+famous+shrines+in+japan&dq=Visiting+famous+shrines+in+japan&client=firefox-a ''Visiting Famous Shrines in Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby-Fane Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/oclc/1030156 OCLC 1030156] {{refend}} {{Shinto shrine}} {{coord|34.9737|N|135.914|E|source:kolossus-jawiki|display=title}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Takebe Shrine}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Shiga Prefecture]] {{Shinto-stub}} [[ja:建部大社]]

''Visiting Famous Shrines in Japan,'' pp. 308–323. ==History== The shrine was established in the 43rd year of the reign of [[Emperor Keiko]].Ponsonby-Fane, Richarc. 1964). ''Visiting Shrines,'' p. 313. The shrine became the object of Imperial patronage during the early [[Heian period]] and it was accorded some attention during the [[Kamakura period]]; however, the Shrine was amongst the many which failed to prosper during the difficult years of the [[Sengoku period]].Ponsonby-Fane, ''Visiting Shrines,'' pp. 320-321. * '''1868''' (''Meiji 1, 9th month''): When Emperor Meiji mad his first visit to [[Edo]], he dispatched an emissary to the shrine with a''heikaku'' for the kami.Ponsonby-Farne, ''Visiting Shrines,'' p. 321. (1959). {{Infobox Shinto shrine | name = Takebe taisha
建部大社 | image = Takebe Taisha2.jpg | caption = Takebe taisha | type = | dedication = | founded = | closed = | founder = | priest = | address = | phone = | website = }} {{nihongo|'''Takebe taisha'''|建部大社|}} is a [[Shinto]] shrine located at [[Ōtsu, Shiga|Ōtsu]] in [[Shiga Prefecture]], [[Japan]]. The shrine is also known in Japanese (esp. formerly) as {{nihongo3||建部神社|Takebe-jinja}}.Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1964). ''Visiting Famous Shrines in Japan,'' pp. 308–323. ==History== The shrine was established in the 43rd year of the reign of the legendary [[Emperor Keiko]], shortly after the death of his son [[Yamato Takeru]].Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1964). ''Visiting Shrines,'' p. 313. The shrine became the object of Imperial patronage during the early [[Heian period]] and it was accorded some attention during the [[Kamakura period]]; however, the Shrine was amongst the many which failed to prosper during the difficult years of the [[Sengoku period]].Ponsonby-Fane, ''Visiting Shrines,'' pp. 320-321. * '''755''' (''[[Tenpyō-shōhō]] 7, 3rd month''): The shrine was moved to its current location on orders of [[Empress Kōken]].Ponsonby-Fane, ''Visiting Shrines,'' p. 316. * '''1190''' (''[[Kenkyū]]1, 11th month''): [[Ashikaga Yoritomo]] visited the shrine on his way to Kyoto.Ponsonby-Farne, ''Visiting Shrines,'' p. 321. * '''1233''' (''[[Jōei]] 2''): [[Ashikaga Yoritsune]] completedly rebuilt the structures of the shrine. * '''1868''' (''Meiji 1, 9th month''): When [[Emperor Meiji]] made his first visit to [[Edo]], he dispatched an emissary to the shrine with a''heikaku'' for the kami. Takebe was designated as the chief Shinto shrine (''[[ichinomiya]]'') for the former [[Ōmi province]]. [http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/images/uploads/EOS070712Ab.pdf "Nationwide List of ''Ichinomiya''," p. 1.]; retrieved 2011-08-09 From 1871 through 1946, the Takebe was officially designated one of the {{nihongo|''[[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines|Kanpei-taisha]]''|官幣大社}},Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 124. meaning that it stood in the first rank of government supported shrines which were especially venerated by the imperial family. ==Festivals== Annual festivals at Takebe include modern events like the ''Reisai'' in mid-AprilPonsonby-Farne, ''Visiting Shrines,'' p. 322. and older rites such as * ''Gokokusai'' or ''Gokoku no matsuri'' in early February.Ponsonby-Farne, ''Visiting Shrines,'' pp. 322-323. * ''Funayiki no matsuri'' in mid AugustPonsonby-Farne, ''Visiting Shrines,'' p. 323. * ''Chinkwa-sai'' in late November.Ponsonby-Farne, ''Visiting Shrines,'' pp. 323-324. == See also== * [[List of Shinto shrines]] * [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]] * [[Ōmi Maru]] ==Notes== {{reflist|2}} ==References== {{refbegin}} * [[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane], Richard]]. (1959). [http://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&q=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan&dq=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan&client=firefox-a&pgis=1 ''The Imperial House of Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/194887 OCLC 194887] * ____________. (1964). [http://books.google.com/books?id=_6rXAAAAMAAJ&q=Visiting+famous+shrines+in+japan&dq=Visiting+famous+shrines+in+japan&client=firefox-a ''Visiting Famous Shrines in Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby-Fane Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/oclc/1030156 OCLC 1030156] {{refend}} {{Shinto shrine}} {{coord|34.9737|N|135.914|E|source:kolossus-jawiki|display=title}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Takebe Shrine}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Shiga Prefecture]] {{Shinto-stub}} [[ja:建部大社]]

{{Infobox Shinto shrine | name = Takebe taisha
建部大社 | image = Takebe Taisha2.jpg | caption = Takebe taisha | type = | dedication = | founded = | closed = | founder = | priest = | address = | phone = | website = }} {{nihongo|'''Takebe taisha'''|建部大社|}} is a [[Shinto]] shrine located at [[Ōtsu, Shiga|Ōtsu]] in [[Shiga Prefecture]], [[Japan]]. The shrine is also known in Japanese (esp. formerly) as {{nihongo3||建部神社|Takebe-jinja}}.Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1964). ''Visiting Famous Shrines in Japan,'' pp. 308–323. ==History== The shrine was established in the 43rd year of the reign of the legendary [[Emperor Keiko]], shortly after the death of his son [[Yamato Takeru]].Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1964). ''Visiting Shrines,'' p. 313. The shrine became the object of Imperial patronage during the early [[Heian period]] and it was accorded some attention during the [[Kamakura period]]; however, the Shrine was amongst the many which failed to prosper during the difficult years of the [[Sengoku period]].Ponsonby-Fane, ''Visiting Shrines,'' pp. 320-321. * '''755''' (''[[Tenpyō-shōhō]] 7, 3rd month''): The shrine was moved to its current location on orders of [[Empress Kōken]].Ponsonby-Fane, ''Visiting Shrines,'' p. 316. * '''1190''' (''[[Kenkyū]]1, 11th month''): [[Minamoto no Yoritomo]] visited the shrine on his way to Kyoto.Ponsonby-Farne, ''Visiting Shrines,'' p. 321. * '''1233''' (''[[Jōei]] 2''): [[Kujō Yoritsune]] completedly rebuilt the structures of the shrine. * '''1868''' (''Meiji 1, 9th month''): When [[Emperor Meiji]] made his first visit to [[Edo]], he dispatched an emissary to the shrine with a''heikaku'' for the kami. Takebe was designated as the chief Shinto shrine (''[[ichinomiya]]'') for the former [[Ōmi province]]. [http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/images/uploads/EOS070712Ab.pdf "Nationwide List of ''Ichinomiya''," p. 1.]; retrieved 2011-08-09 From 1871 through 1946, the Takebe was officially designated one of the {{nihongo|''[[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines|Kanpei-taisha]]''|官幣大社}},Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 124. meaning that it stood in the first rank of government supported shrines which were especially venerated by the imperial family. ==Festivals== Annual festivals at Takebe include modern events like the ''Reisai'' in mid-AprilPonsonby-Farne, ''Visiting Shrines,'' p. 322. and older rites such as * ''Gokokusai'' or ''Gokoku no matsuri'' in early February.Ponsonby-Farne, ''Visiting Shrines,'' pp. 322-323. * ''Funayiki no matsuri'' in mid AugustPonsonby-Farne, ''Visiting Shrines,'' p. 323. * ''Chinkwa-sai'' in late November.Ponsonby-Farne, ''Visiting Shrines,'' pp. 323-324. == See also== * [[List of Shinto shrines]] * [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]] * [[Ōmi Maru]] ==Notes== {{reflist|2}} ==References== {{refbegin}} * [[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane], Richard]]. (1959). [http://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&q=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan&dq=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan&client=firefox-a&pgis=1 ''The Imperial House of Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/194887 OCLC 194887] * ____________. (1964). [http://books.google.com/books?id=_6rXAAAAMAAJ&q=Visiting+famous+shrines+in+japan&dq=Visiting+famous+shrines+in+japan&client=firefox-a ''Visiting Famous Shrines in Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby-Fane Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/oclc/1030156 OCLC 1030156] {{refend}} {{Shinto shrine}} {{coord|34.9737|N|135.914|E|source:kolossus-jawiki|display=title}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Takebe Shrine}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Shiga Prefecture]] {{Shinto-stub}} [[ja:建部大社]]

[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/images/uploads/EOS070712Ab.pdf "Nationwide List of ''Ichinomiya''," p. 1.]; retrieved 2011-08-09 From 1871 through 1946, the Takebe was officially designated one of the {{nihongo|''[[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines|Kanpei-taisha]]''|官幣大社}},Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 124. meaning that it stood in the first rank of government supported shrines which were especially venerated by the imperial family. ==Festivals== Annual festivals at Takebe include modern events like the ''Reisai'' in mid-AprilPonsonby-Farne, ''Visiting Shrines,'' p. 322. and older rites such as * ''Gokokusai'' or ''Gokoku no matsuri'' in early February.Ponsonby-Farne, ''Visiting Shrines,'' pp. 322-323. * ''Funayiki no matsuri'' in mid AugustPonsonby-Farne, ''Visiting Shrines,'' p. 323. * ''Chinkwa-sai'' in late November.Ponsonby-Farne, ''Visiting Shrines,'' pp. 323-324. == See also== * [[List of Shinto shrines]] * [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]] * [[Ōmi Maru]] ==Notes== {{reflist|2}} ==References== {{refbegin}} * [[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane, Richard]]. ''Visiting Famous Shrines in Japan,'' pp. 308–323. ==History== The shrine was established in the 43rd year of the reign of the legendary [[Emperor Keiko]], shortly after the death of his son [[Yamato Takeru]].Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1964). ''Visiting Shrines,'' p. 313. The shrine became the object of Imperial patronage during the early [[Heian period]] and it was accorded some attention during the [[Kamakura period]]; however, the Shrine was amongst the many which failed to prosper during the difficult years of the [[Sengoku period]].Ponsonby-Fane, ''Visiting Shrines,'' pp. 320-321. * '''755''' (''[[Tenpyō-shōhō]] 7, 3rd month''): The shrine was moved to its current location on orders of [[Empress Kōken]].Ponsonby-Fane, ''Visiting Shrines,'' p. 316. * '''1190''' (''[[Kenkyū]]1, 11th month''): [[Minamoto no Yoritomo]] visited the shrine on his way to Kyoto.Ponsonby-Farne, ''Visiting Shrines,'' p. 321. * '''1233''' (''[[Jōei]] 2''): [[Kujō Yoritsune]] completedly rebuilt the structures of the shrine. * '''1868''' (''Meiji 1, 9th month''): When [[Emperor Meiji]] made his first visit to [[Edo]], he dispatched an emissary to the shrine with a''heikaku'' for the kami. Takebe was designated as the chief Shinto shrine (''[[ichinomiya]]'') for the former [[Ōmi province]]. [http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/images/uploads/EOS070712Ab.pdf "Nationwide List of ''Ichinomiya''," p. 1.]; retrieved 2011-08-09 From 1871 through 1946, the Takebe was officially designated one of the {{nihongo|''[[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines|Kanpei-taisha]]''|官幣大社}},Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 124. meaning that it stood in the first rank of government supported shrines which were especially venerated by the imperial family. ==Festivals== Annual festivals at Takebe include modern events like the ''Reisai'' in mid-AprilPonsonby-Farne, ''Visiting Shrines,'' p. 322. and older rites such as * ''Gokokusai'' or ''Gokoku no matsuri'' in early February.Ponsonby-Farne, ''Visiting Shrines,'' pp. 322-323. * ''Funayiki no matsuri'' in mid AugustPonsonby-Farne, ''Visiting Shrines,'' p. 323. * ''Chinkwa-sai'' in late November.Ponsonby-Farne, ''Visiting Shrines,'' pp. 323-324. == See also== * [[List of Shinto shrines]] * [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]] * [[Ōmi Maru]] ==Notes== {{Reflist|2}} ==References== {{Refbegin}} * [[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane, Richard]]. (1959). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&q=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan&dq=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan&client=firefox-a&pgis=1 ''The Imperial House of Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/194887 OCLC 194887] * ____________. (1964). [https://books.google.com/books?id=_6rXAAAAMAAJ&q=Visiting+famous+shrines+in+japan&dq=Visiting+famous+shrines+in+japan&client=firefox-a ''Visiting Famous Shrines in Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby-Fane Memorial Society. {{OCLC|1030156}} {{Refend}} {{Shinto shrine}} {{Authority control}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Takebe Shrine}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Shiga Prefecture]] {{Shinto-stub}}

[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/images/uploads/EOS070712Ab.pdf "Nationwide List of ''Ichinomiya''," p. 1.]; retrieved 2011-08-09 From 1871 through 1946, the Takebe was officially designated one of the {{nihongo|''[[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines|Kanpei-taisha]]''|官幣大社}},Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). (1959). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&q=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan&dq=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan&client=firefox-a&pgis=1 ''The Imperial House of Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/194887 OCLC 194887] * ____________. (1964). {{Short description|Shinto shrine in Ōtsu, Shiga, Japan}} {{Use dmy dates|date=November 2019}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Takebe Taisha
建部大社 | image = Takebe-shrine.jpg | caption = ''Heiden'' of Takebe Taisha and three sacred sugi trees {{Infobox mapframe|tyep=shape|zoom=14|frame-width=240}} | map_type = Japan Shiga Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|34|58|24.6|N|135|54|48.62|E|type:landmark_region:JP|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = [[Yamato Takeru]]
[[Ōkuninushi|Ōkuninmushi no Mikoto]] | founded_by = [[Emperor Keiko]] | established = c.[[Kofun period]] | date_destroyed = | location = 16-1, Jinryō 1-chōme, Ōtsu, Shiga, Japan | website = {{Official|1=http://takebetaisha.jp/}} | architecture_style = ''[[Nagare-zukuri]]'' | festival = ''Reitaisai'' (15 April) | leadership = | footnotes ={{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=pink|text align=center|[[Important Cultural Properties of Japan|Important Cultural Property]]}} }} {{nihongo|'''Takebe Shrine'''|建部大社| Takebe Taisha}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in the city of [[Ōtsu, Shiga|Ōtsu]], [[Shiga Prefecture]], [[Japan]]. It is the ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of former [[Ōmi Province]].[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/images/uploads/EOS070712Ab.pdf "Nationwide List of ''Ichinomiya''," p. 1.]; retrieved 2011-08-010 The main ''[[kami]]'' enshrined are [[Ōkuninushi]] and [[Yamato Takeru]]. The shrine's main festival is held annually on April 15. {{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=Shokoku jinja Ichinomiya Ninomiya San'nomiya |date=2015 |publisher= Yamakawa shuppansha |isbn=4634150867 |language=Japanese}} ==Enshrined ''kami''== The ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at Takebe Taisha are: * {{nihongo|'''[[Yamato Takeru]]'''| 日本武尊|}}, folk hero and the son of [[Emperor Keikō]] * {{nihongo|'''[[Ōkuninushi]]'''| 大己貴命|}}, the god of nation-building, agriculture, medicine, and protective magic ==History== According to the shrine's legend, its foundation has strong connections with the [[Yamato Takeru]] mythology. Following Yamato Takeru's death, his wife Princess Futaji resided at a place called Chigusatake, Takebe-go in what is now [[Kanzaki District, Shiga]], together with their son Takebe Inai Butsumei. A shrine was erected in that location 43rd year of the reign of the legendary [[Emperor Keikō]], who was Yamato Takeru's father. The place name "Takebe" is thought to come from the name "Takeru", and is also found in other places connected with theYamato Takeru myth. Later, under the reign of [[Emperor Tenmu]], the shrine was relocated to Seto, in [[Kurita District, Shiga|Kurita District]] and became the guardian shrine for Ōmi Province. However, in 755 the shrine was moved to its current location on orders of [[Empress Kōken]],Ponsonby-Fane, ''Visiting Shrines,'' p. 316.and [[Ōkuninushi]] was also installed as a deity at the shrine as a ''[[bunrei]]'' of [[Ōmiwa Shrine]]. The shrine appears in various [[Heian period]] documents, including the ''[[Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku]]'', ''[[Ruijū Kokushi]]'' and the ''[[Engishiki]]'', and came to be regarded as the ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of Ōmi Province.{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku `Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=4569669301 |language=Japanese}} When [[Minamoto no Yoritomo]] was sent to exile in [[Izu Province|Izu]] following the defeat of the [[Genji clan]] in the [[Heiji Rebellion]], he stopped to pray for the revival of the Genji at this shrine. In 1190, when the [[Heike clan]] was overthrown, he again prayed at this shrine in thanksgiving, and the shrine became a popular pilgrimage destination for good fortune. Ponsonby-Farne, ''Visiting Shrines,'' p. 321. The shrine was rebuilt in 1233 by [[Kujō Yoritsune]]. * '''1233''' (''[[Jōei]] 2''): [[Kujō Yoritsune]] completely rebuilt the structures of the shrine. In 1868, when [[Emperor Meiji]] made his first visit to [[Edo]], he dispatched an emissary to the shrine with a ''heikaku'' for the kami. In 1871, the shrine was listed under the name {{nihongo|'''Takebe Jinja'''|建部神社||}} as a prefectural shrine under the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines]] under [[State Shinto]]. In 1885, it was promoted to a {{nihongo|Imperial shrine, 2nd rank|官幣中社|Kanpei-chūsha}} and in 1900 to a {{nihongo|Imperial shrine, 1st rank|官幣大社|Kanpei-taisha}}.Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 124.. In 1945, the shrine was used as the design for the first [[1000 yen note]] issued. In 1948 the shrine's name was officially changed to "Takebe Taisha".{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku `Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=4582945619 |language=Japanese}} The shrine is 15 minutes on foot from [[Karahashimae Station]] on the [[Keihan Electric Railway]] [[Ishiyama Sakamoto Line]].{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku `Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=4582945619 |language=Japanese}} ==Gallery== Takebe Taisha1.jpg|First Torii Takebe Taisha honden.jpg|Honden Takebe Taisha homotsuden.jpg|Museum Takebe Taisha ishitoro.jpg|Stone lantern (ICP) Takebe Taisha2.jpg|Second Torii ==Cultural Properties== ===Important Cultural Properties=== *[[Tōrō]] stone lantern, dated 1270. {{cite web |url= https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/6834|title=石燈籠|trans-title=Ishi Tōrō|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} *Wooden Statue of a Female Deity (2 pcs), [[Heian period]]. {{cite web |url= https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/3192|title=木造女神坐像|trans-title=Mokuzō nyoshinzazō|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} ==See also== *[[List of Shinto shrines]] *[[Ichinomiya]] ==References== {{reflist}} ==External links== {{commons category|Takebe Taisha}} *[http://takebetaisha.jp/ Official website] *[https://otsu.or.jp/en/thingstodo/spot49 Biwako-Otsu Travel Guide] {{Shinto shrine}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Shiga Prefecture]] [[Category:Ichinomiya]] [[Category: Ōmi Province]] [[Category:Ōtsu, Shiga]]

Ponsonby-Farne, ''Visiting Shrines,'' p. 321. The shrine was rebuilt in 1233 by [[Kujō Yoritsune]]. * '''1233''' (''[[Jōei]] 2''): [[Kujō Yoritsune]] completely rebuilt the structures of the shrine. In 1868, when [[Emperor Meiji]] made his first visit to [[Edo]], he dispatched an emissary to the shrine with a ''heikaku'' for the kami. In 1871, the shrine was listed under the name {{nihongo|'''Takebe Jinja'''|建部神社||}} as a prefectural shrine under the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines]] under [[State Shinto]]. In 1885, it was promoted to a {{nihongo|Imperial shrine, 2nd rank|官幣中社|Kanpei-chūsha}} and in 1900 to a {{nihongo|Imperial shrine, 1st rank|官幣大社|Kanpei-taisha}}.Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 124. In 1945, the shrine was used as the design for the first [[1000 yen note]] issued. In 1948 the shrine's name was officially changed to "Takebe Taisha".{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku `Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=4582945619 |language=Japanese}} The shrine is 15 minutes on foot from [[Karahashimae Station]] on the [[Keihan Electric Railway]] [[Ishiyama Sakamoto Line]].{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku `Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=4582945619 |language=Japanese}} ==Gallery== Takebe Taisha1.jpg|First Torii Takebe Taisha honden.jpg|Honden Takebe Taisha homotsuden.jpg|Museum Takebe Taisha ishitoro.jpg|Stone lantern (ICP) Takebe Taisha2.jpg|Second Torii ==Cultural Properties== ===Important Cultural Properties=== *[[Tōrō]] stone lantern, dated 1270. {{cite web |url= https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/6834|title=石燈籠|trans-title=Ishi Tōrō|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} *Wooden Statue of a Female Deity (2 pcs), [[Heian period]]. {{cite web |url= https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/3192|title=木造女神坐像|trans-title=Mokuzō nyoshinzazō|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} ==See also== *[[List of Shinto shrines]] *[[Ichinomiya]] ==References== {{reflist}} ==External links== {{commons category|Takebe Taisha}} *[http://takebetaisha.jp/ Official website] *[https://otsu.or.jp/en/thingstodo/spot49 Biwako-Otsu Travel Guide] {{Shinto shrine}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Shiga Prefecture]] [[Category:Ichinomiya]] [[Category: Ōmi Province]] [[Category:Ōtsu]]

{{Short description|Shinto shrine in Ōtsu, Shiga, Japan}} {{Use dmy dates|date=November 2019}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Takebe Taisha
建部大社 | image = Takebe-shrine.jpg | caption = ''Heiden'' of Takebe Taisha and three sacred sugi trees {{Infobox mapframe|tyep=shape|zoom=14|frame-width=240}} | map_type = Japan Shiga Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|34|58|24.6|N|135|54|48.62|E|type:landmark_region:JP|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = [[Yamato Takeru]]
[[Ōkuninushi|Ōkuninmushi no Mikoto]] | founded_by = [[Emperor Keiko]] | established = c.[[Kofun period]] | date_destroyed = | location = 16-1, Jinryō 1-chōme, Ōtsu, Shiga, Japan | website = {{Official|1=http://takebetaisha.jp/}} | architecture_style = ''[[Nagare-zukuri]]'' | festival = ''Reitaisai'' (15 April) | leadership = | footnotes ={{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=pink|text align=center|[[Important Cultural Properties of Japan|Important Cultural Property]]}} }} {{nihongo|'''Takebe Shrine'''|建部大社| Takebe Taisha}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in the city of [[Ōtsu, Shiga|Ōtsu]], [[Shiga Prefecture]], [[Japan]]. It is the ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of former [[Ōmi Province]].[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/images/uploads/EOS070712Ab.pdf "Nationwide List of ''Ichinomiya''," p. 1.]; retrieved 2011-08-010 The main ''[[kami]]'' enshrined are [[Ōkuninushi]] and [[Yamato Takeru]]. The shrine's main festival is held annually on April 15. {{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=Shokoku jinja Ichinomiya Ninomiya San'nomiya |date=2015 |publisher= Yamakawa shuppansha |isbn=4634150867 |language=Japanese}} ==Enshrined ''kami''== The ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at Takebe Taisha are: * {{nihongo|'''[[Yamato Takeru]]'''| 日本武尊|}}, folk hero and the son of [[Emperor Keikō]] * {{nihongo|'''[[Ōkuninushi]]'''| 大己貴命|}}, the god of nation-building, agriculture, medicine, and protective magic [[Category:Beppo Shrines]] ==History== According to the shrine's legend, its foundation has strong connections with the [[Yamato Takeru]] mythology. Following Yamato Takeru's death, his wife Princess Futaji resided at a place called Chigusatake, Takebe-go in what is now [[Kanzaki District, Shiga]], together with their son Takebe Inai Butsumei. A shrine was erected in that location 43rd year of the reign of the legendary [[Emperor Keikō]], who was Yamato Takeru's father. The place name "Takebe" is thought to come from the name "Takeru", and is also found in other places connected with theYamato Takeru myth. Later, under the reign of [[Emperor Tenmu]], the shrine was relocated to Seto, in [[Kurita District, Shiga|Kurita District]] and became the guardian shrine for Ōmi Province. However, in 755 the shrine was moved to its current location on orders of [[Empress Kōken]],Ponsonby-Fane, ''Visiting Shrines,'' p. 316.and [[Ōkuninushi]] was also installed as a deity at the shrine as a ''[[bunrei]]'' of [[Ōmiwa Shrine]]. The shrine appears in various [[Heian period]] documents, including the ''[[Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku]]'', ''[[Ruijū Kokushi]]'' and the ''[[Engishiki]]'', and came to be regarded as the ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of Ōmi Province.{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku `Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=4569669301 |language=Japanese}} When [[Minamoto no Yoritomo]] was sent to exile in [[Izu Province|Izu]] following the defeat of the [[Genji clan]] in the [[Heiji Rebellion]], he stopped to pray for the revival of the Genji at this shrine. In 1190, when the [[Heike clan]] was overthrown, he again prayed at this shrine in thanksgiving, and the shrine became a popular pilgrimage destination for good fortune. Ponsonby-Farne, ''Visiting Shrines,'' p. 321. The shrine was rebuilt in 1233 by [[Kujō Yoritsune]]. * '''1233''' (''[[Jōei]] 2''): [[Kujō Yoritsune]] completely rebuilt the structures of the shrine. In 1868, when [[Emperor Meiji]] made his first visit to [[Edo]], he dispatched an emissary to the shrine with a ''heikaku'' for the kami. In 1871, the shrine was listed under the name {{nihongo|'''Takebe Jinja'''|建部神社||}} as a prefectural shrine under the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines]] under [[State Shinto]]. In 1885, it was promoted to a {{nihongo|Imperial shrine, 2nd rank|官幣中社|Kanpei-chūsha}} and in 1900 to a {{nihongo|Imperial shrine, 1st rank|官幣大社|Kanpei-taisha}}.Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 124. In 1945, the shrine was used as the design for the first [[1000 yen note]] issued. In 1948 the shrine's name was officially changed to "Takebe Taisha".{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku `Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=4582945619 |language=Japanese}} The shrine is 15 minutes on foot from [[Karahashimae Station]] on the [[Keihan Electric Railway]] [[Ishiyama Sakamoto Line]].{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku `Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=4582945619 |language=Japanese}} ==Gallery== Takebe Taisha1.jpg|First Torii Takebe Taisha honden.jpg|Honden Takebe Taisha homotsuden.jpg|Museum Takebe Taisha ishitoro.jpg|Stone lantern (ICP) Takebe Taisha2.jpg|Second Torii ==Cultural Properties== ===Important Cultural Properties=== *[[Tōrō]] stone lantern, dated 1270. {{cite web |url= https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/6834|title=石燈籠|trans-title=Ishi Tōrō|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} *Wooden Statue of a Female Deity (2 pcs), [[Heian period]]. {{cite web |url= https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/3192|title=木造女神坐像|trans-title=Mokuzō nyoshinzazō|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} ==See also== *[[List of Shinto shrines]] *[[Ichinomiya]] ==References== {{reflist}} ==External links== {{commons category|Takebe Taisha}} *[http://takebetaisha.jp/ Official website] *[https://otsu.or.jp/en/thingstodo/spot49 Biwako-Otsu Travel Guide] {{Shinto shrine}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Shiga Prefecture]] [[Category:Ichinomiya]] [[Category: Ōmi Province]] [[Category:Ōtsu]]

However, in 755 the shrine was moved to its current location on orders of [[Empress Kōken]],Ponsonby-Fane, ''Visiting Shrines,'' p. 316.and [[Ōkuninushi]] was also installed as a deity at the shrine as a ''[[bunrei]]'' of [[Ōmiwa Shrine]]. The shrine appears in various [[Heian period]] documents, including the ''[[Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku]]'', ''[[Ruijū Kokushi]]'' and the ''[[Engishiki]]'', and came to be regarded as the ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of Ōmi Province.{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku `Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=4569669301 |language=Japanese}} When [[Minamoto no Yoritomo]] was sent to exile in [[Izu Province|Izu]] following the defeat of the [[Genji clan]] in the [[Heiji Rebellion]], he stopped to pray for the revival of the Genji at this shrine. In 1190, when the [[Heike clan]] was overthrown, he again prayed at this shrine in thanksgiving, and the shrine became a popular pilgrimage destination for good fortune. Ponsonby-Farne, ''Visiting Shrines,'' p. 321. The shrine was rebuilt in 1233 by [[Kujō Yoritsune]]. * '''1233''' (''[[Jōei]] 2''): [[Kujō Yoritsune]] completely rebuilt the structures of the shrine. In 1868, when [[Emperor Meiji]] made his first visit to [[Edo]], he dispatched an emissary to the shrine with a ''heikaku'' for the kami. In 1871, the shrine was listed under the name {{nihongo|'''Takebe Jinja'''|建部神社||}} as a prefectural shrine under the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines]] under [[State Shinto]]. In 1885, it was promoted to a {{nihongo|Imperial shrine, 2nd rank|官幣中社|Kanpei-chūsha}} and in 1900 to a {{nihongo|Imperial shrine, 1st rank|官幣大社|Kanpei-taisha}}.Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 124. In 1945, the shrine was used as the design for the first [[1000 yen note]] issued. In 1948 the shrine's name was officially changed to "Takebe Taisha".{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku `Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=4582945619 |language=Japanese}} The shrine is 15 minutes on foot from [[Karahashimae Station]] on the [[Keihan Electric Railway]] [[Ishiyama Sakamoto Line]].{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku `Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=4582945619 |language=Japanese}} ==Gallery== Takebe Taisha1.jpg|First Torii Takebe Taisha honden.jpg|Honden Takebe Taisha homotsuden.jpg|Museum Takebe Taisha ishitoro.jpg|Stone lantern (ICP) Takebe Taisha2.jpg|Second Torii ==Cultural Properties== ===Important Cultural Properties=== *[[Tōrō]] stone lantern, dated 1270. {{cite web |url= https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/6834|title=石燈籠|trans-title=Ishi Tōrō|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} *Wooden Statue of a Female Deity (2 pcs), [[Heian period]]. {{cite web |url= https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/3192|title=木造女神坐像|trans-title=Mokuzō nyoshinzazō|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} ==See also== *[[List of Shinto shrines]] *[[Ichinomiya]] ==References== {{reflist}} ==External links== {{commons category|Takebe Taisha}} *[http://takebetaisha.jp/ Official website] *[https://otsu.or.jp/en/thingstodo/spot49 Biwako-Otsu Travel Guide] {{Shinto shrine}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Shiga Prefecture]] [[Category:Ichinomiya]] [[Category: Ōmi Province]] [[Category:Ōtsu]]

{{Short description|Shinto shrine in Ōtsu, Shiga, Japan}} {{Use dmy dates|date=November 2019}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Takebe Taisha
建部大社 | image = Takebe-shrine.jpg | caption = ''Heiden'' of Takebe Taisha and three sacred sugi trees {{Infobox mapframe|tyep=shape|zoom=14|frame-width=240}} | map_type = Japan Shiga Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|34|58|24.6|N|135|54|48.62|E|type:landmark_region:JP|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = [[Yamato Takeru]]
[[Ōkuninushi|Ōkuninmushi no Mikoto]] | founded_by = [[Emperor Keiko]] | established = c.[[Kofun period]] | date_destroyed = | location = 16-1, Jinryō 1-chōme, Ōtsu, Shiga, Japan | website = {{Official|1=http://takebetaisha.jp/}} | architecture_style = ''[[Nagare-zukuri]]'' | festival = ''Reitaisai'' (15 April) | leadership = | footnotes ={{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=pink|text align=center|[[Important Cultural Properties of Japan|Important Cultural Property]]}} }} {{nihongo|'''Takebe Shrine'''|建部大社| Takebe Taisha}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in the city of [[Ōtsu, Shiga|Ōtsu]], [[Shiga Prefecture]], [[Japan]]. It is the ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of former [[Ōmi Province]].[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/images/uploads/EOS070712Ab.pdf "Nationwide List of ''Ichinomiya''," p. 1.]; retrieved 2011-08-010 The main ''[[kami]]'' enshrined are [[Ōkuninushi]] and [[Yamato Takeru]]. The shrine's main festival is held annually on April 15.{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=Shokoku jinja Ichinomiya Ninomiya San'nomiya |date=2015 |publisher= Yamakawa shuppansha |isbn=4634150867 |language=Japanese}} ==Enshrined ''kami''== The ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at Takebe Taisha are: * {{nihongo|'''[[Yamato Takeru]]'''| 日本武尊|}}, folk hero and the son of [[Emperor Keikō]] * {{nihongo|'''[[Ōkuninushi]]'''| 大己貴命|}}, the god of nation-building, agriculture, medicine, and protective magic ==History== According to the shrine's legend, its foundation has strong connections with the [[Yamato Takeru]] mythology. Following Yamato Takeru's death, his wife Princess Futaji resided at a place called Chigusatake, Takebe-go in what is now [[Kanzaki District, Shiga]], together with their son Takebe Inai Butsumei. A shrine was erected in that location 43rd year of the reign of the legendary [[Emperor Keikō]], who was Yamato Takeru's father. The place name "Takebe" is thought to come from the name "Takeru", and is also found in other places connected with theYamato Takeru myth. Later, under the reign of [[Emperor Tenmu]], the shrine was relocated to Seto, in [[Kurita District, Shiga|Kurita District]] and became the guardian shrine for Ōmi Province. However, in 755 the shrine was moved to its current location on orders of [[Empress Kōken]],Ponsonby-Fane, ''Visiting Shrines,'' p. 316. and [[Ōkuninushi]] was also installed as a deity at the shrine as a ''[[bunrei]]'' of [[Ōmiwa Shrine]]. The shrine appears in various [[Heian period]] documents, including the ''[[Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku]]'', ''[[Ruijū Kokushi]]'' and the ''[[Engishiki]]'', and came to be regarded as the ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of Ōmi Province.{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku `Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=4569669301 |language=Japanese}} When [[Minamoto no Yoritomo]] was sent to exile in [[Izu Province|Izu]] following the defeat of the [[Genji clan]] in the [[Heiji Rebellion]], he stopped to pray for the revival of the Genji at this shrine. In 1190, when the [[Heike clan]] was overthrown, he again prayed at this shrine in thanksgiving, and the shrine became a popular pilgrimage destination for good fortune.Ponsonby-Farne, ''Visiting Shrines,'' p. 321. The shrine was rebuilt in 1233 by [[Kujō Yoritsune]]. * '''1233''' (''[[Jōei]] 2''): [[Kujō Yoritsune]] completely rebuilt the structures of the shrine. In 1868, when [[Emperor Meiji]] made his first visit to [[Edo]], he dispatched an emissary to the shrine with a ''heikaku'' for the kami. In 1871, the shrine was listed under the name {{nihongo|'''Takebe Jinja'''|建部神社||}} as a prefectural shrine under the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines]] under [[State Shinto]]. In 1885, it was promoted to a {{nihongo|Imperial shrine, 2nd rank|官幣中社|Kanpei-chūsha}} and in 1900 to a {{nihongo|Imperial shrine, 1st rank|官幣大社|Kanpei-taisha}}.Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 124. In 1945, the shrine was used as the design for the first [[1000 yen note]] issued. In 1948 the shrine's name was officially changed to "Takebe Taisha".{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku `Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=4582945619 |language=Japanese}} The shrine is 15 minutes on foot from [[Karahashimae Station]] on the [[Keihan Electric Railway]] [[Ishiyama Sakamoto Line]]. ==Gallery== Takebe Taisha1.jpg|First Torii Takebe Taisha honden.jpg|Honden Takebe Taisha homotsuden.jpg|Museum Takebe Taisha ishitoro.jpg|Stone lantern (ICP) Takebe Taisha2.jpg|Second Torii ==Cultural Properties== ===Important Cultural Properties=== *[[Tōrō]] stone lantern, dated 1270.{{cite web |url= https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/6834|title=石燈籠|trans-title=Ishi Tōrō|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} *Wooden Statue of a Female Deity (2 pcs), [[Heian period]].{{cite web |url= https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/3192|title=木造女神坐像|trans-title=Mokuzō nyoshinzazō|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} ==See also== *[[List of Shinto shrines]] *[[Ichinomiya]] ==References== {{reflist}} ==External links== {{commons category|Takebe Taisha}} *[http://takebetaisha.jp/ Official website] *[https://otsu.or.jp/en/thingstodo/spot49 Biwako-Otsu Travel Guide] {{Shinto shrine}} [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Kanpei-taisha]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Shiga Prefecture]] [[Category:Ichinomiya]] [[Category:Ōmi Province]] [[Category:Ōtsu]]

It is the ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of former [[Ōmi Province]].[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/images/uploads/EOS070712Ab.pdf "Nationwide List of ''Ichinomiya''," p. 1.]; retrieved 2011-08-010 The main ''[[kami]]'' enshrined are [[Ōkuninushi]] and [[Yamato Takeru]]. The shrine's main festival is held annually on April 15.{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=Shokoku jinja Ichinomiya Ninomiya San'nomiya |date=2015 |publisher= Yamakawa shuppansha |isbn=978-4634150867 |language=Japanese}} ==Enshrined ''kami''== The ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at Takebe Taisha are: * {{nihongo|'''[[Yamato Takeru]]'''| 日本武尊|}}, folk hero and the son of [[Emperor Keikō]] * {{nihongo|'''[[Ōkuninushi]]'''| 大己貴命|}}, the god of nation-building, agriculture, medicine, and protective magic ==History== According to the shrine's legend, its foundation has strong connections with the [[Yamato Takeru]] mythology. Following Yamato Takeru's death, his wife Princess Futaji resided at a place called Chigusatake, Takebe-go in what is now [[Kanzaki District, Shiga]], together with their son Takebe Inai Butsumei. A shrine was erected in that location 43rd year of the reign of the legendary [[Emperor Keikō]], who was Yamato Takeru's father. The place name "Takebe" is thought to come from the name "Takeru", and is also found in other places connected with theYamato Takeru myth. Later, under the reign of [[Emperor Tenmu]], the shrine was relocated to Seto, in [[Kurita District, Shiga|Kurita District]] and became the guardian shrine for Ōmi Province. However, in 755 the shrine was moved to its current location on orders of [[Empress Kōken]],Ponsonby-Fane, ''Visiting Shrines,'' p. 316. and [[Ōkuninushi]] was also installed as a deity at the shrine as a ''[[bunrei]]'' of [[Ōmiwa Shrine]]. The shrine appears in various [[Heian period]] documents, including the ''[[Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku]]'', ''[[Ruijū Kokushi]]'' and the ''[[Engishiki]]'', and came to be regarded as the ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of Ōmi Province.{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=978-4569669304 |language=Japanese}} When [[Minamoto no Yoritomo]] was sent to exile in [[Izu Province|Izu]] following the defeat of the [[Genji clan]] in the [[Heiji Rebellion]], he stopped to pray for the revival of the Genji at this shrine. In 1190, when the [[Heike clan]] was overthrown, he again prayed at this shrine in thanksgiving, and the shrine became a popular pilgrimage destination for good fortune.Ponsonby-Farne, ''Visiting Shrines,'' p. 321. The shrine was rebuilt in 1233 by [[Kujō Yoritsune]]. * '''1233''' (''[[Jōei]] 2''): [[Kujō Yoritsune]] completely rebuilt the structures of the shrine. In 1868, when [[Emperor Meiji]] made his first visit to [[Edo]], he dispatched an emissary to the shrine with a ''heikaku'' for the kami. In 1871, the shrine was listed under the name {{nihongo|'''Takebe Jinja'''|建部神社||}} as a prefectural shrine under the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines]] under [[State Shinto]]. In 1885, it was promoted to a {{nihongo|Imperial shrine, 2nd rank|官幣中社|Kanpei-chūsha}} and in 1900 to a {{nihongo|Imperial shrine, 1st rank|官幣大社|Kanpei-taisha}}.Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 124. In 1945, the shrine was used as the design for the first [[1000 yen note]] issued. In 1948 the shrine's name was officially changed to "Takebe Taisha".{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}} The shrine is 15 minutes on foot from [[Karahashimae Station]] on the [[Keihan Electric Railway]] [[Ishiyama Sakamoto Line]]. ==Gallery== Takebe Taisha1.jpg|First Torii Takebe Taisha honden.jpg|Honden Takebe Taisha homotsuden.jpg|Museum Takebe Taisha ishitoro.jpg|Stone lantern (ICP) Takebe Taisha2.jpg|Second Torii ==Cultural Properties== ===Important Cultural Properties=== *[[Tōrō]] stone lantern, dated 1270.{{cite web |url= https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/6834|title=石燈籠|trans-title=Ishi Tōrō|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} *Wooden Statue of a Female Deity (2 pcs), [[Heian period]].{{cite web |url= https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/3192|title=木造女神坐像|trans-title=Mokuzō nyoshinzazō|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} ==See also== *[[List of Shinto shrines]] *[[Ichinomiya]] ==References== {{reflist}} ==External links== {{commons category|Takebe Taisha}} *[http://takebetaisha.jp/ Official website] *[https://otsu.or.jp/en/thingstodo/spot49 Biwako-Otsu Travel Guide] {{Shinto shrine}} [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Kanpei-taisha]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Shiga Prefecture]] [[Category:Ichinomiya]] [[Category:Ōmi Province]] [[Category:Ōtsu]] [[Category:Myōjin Taisha]]

{{Short description|Shinto shrine in Ōtsu, Shiga, Japan}} {{Use dmy dates|date=November 2019}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Takebe Taisha
建部大社 | image = Takebe-shrine.jpg | caption = ''Heiden'' of Takebe Taisha and three sacred sugi trees {{Infobox mapframe|tyep=shape|zoom=14|frame-width=240}} | map_type = Japan Shiga Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|34|58|24.6|N|135|54|48.62|E|type:landmark_region:JP|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = [[Yamato Takeru]]
[[Ōkuninushi|Ōkuninmushi no Mikoto]] | founded_by = [[Emperor Keiko]] | established = c.[[Kofun period]] | date_destroyed = | location = 16-1, Jinryō 1-chōme, Ōtsu, Shiga, Japan | website = {{Official|1=http://takebetaisha.jp/}} | architecture_style = ''[[Nagare-zukuri]]'' | festival = ''Reitaisai'' (15 April) | leadership = | footnotes ={{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=pink|text align=center|[[Important Cultural Properties of Japan|Important Cultural Property]]}} }} {{nihongo|'''Takebe Shrine'''|建部大社| Takebe Taisha}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in the city of [[Ōtsu, Shiga|Ōtsu]], [[Shiga Prefecture]], [[Japan]]. It is the ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of former [[Ōmi Province]].[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/images/uploads/EOS070712Ab.pdf "Nationwide List of ''Ichinomiya''," p. 1.]; retrieved 2011-08-010 The main ''[[kami]]'' enshrined are [[Ōkuninushi]] and [[Yamato Takeru]]. The shrine's main festival is held annually on April 15.{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=Shokoku jinja Ichinomiya Ninomiya San'nomiya |date=2015 |publisher= Yamakawa shuppansha |isbn=978-4634150867 |language=Japanese}} ==Enshrined ''kami''== The ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at Takebe Taisha are: * {{nihongo|'''[[Yamato Takeru]]'''| 日本武尊|}}, folk hero and the son of [[Emperor Keikō]] * {{nihongo|'''[[Ōkuninushi]]'''| 大己貴命|}}, the god of nation-building, agriculture, medicine, and protective magic ==History== According to the shrine's legend, its foundation has strong connections with the [[Yamato Takeru]] mythology. Following Yamato Takeru's death, his wife Princess Futaji resided at a place called Chigusatake, Takebe-go in what is now [[Kanzaki District, Shiga]], together with their son Takebe Inai Butsumei. A shrine was erected in that location 43rd year of the reign of the legendary [[Emperor Keikō]], who was Yamato Takeru's father. The place name "Takebe" is thought to come from the name "Takeru", and is also found in other places connected with theYamato Takeru myth. Later, under the reign of [[Emperor Tenmu]], the shrine was relocated to Seto, in [[Kurita District, Shiga|Kurita District]] and became the guardian shrine for Ōmi Province. However, in 755 the shrine was moved to its current location on orders of [[Empress Kōken]],Ponsonby-Fane, ''Visiting Shrines,'' p. 316. and [[Ōkuninushi]] was also installed as a deity at the shrine as a ''[[bunrei]]'' of [[Ōmiwa Shrine]]. The shrine appears in various [[Heian period]] documents, including the ''[[Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku]]'', ''[[Ruijū Kokushi]]'' and the ''[[Engishiki]]'', and came to be regarded as the ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of Ōmi Province.{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=978-4569669304 |language=Japanese}} When [[Minamoto no Yoritomo]] was sent to exile in [[Izu Province|Izu]] following the defeat of the [[Genji clan]] in the [[Heiji Rebellion]], he stopped to pray for the revival of the Genji at this shrine. In 1190, when the [[Heike clan]] was overthrown, he again prayed at this shrine in thanksgiving, and the shrine became a popular pilgrimage destination for good fortune.Ponsonby-Farne, ''Visiting Shrines,'' p. 321. The shrine was rebuilt in 1233 by [[Kujō Yoritsune]]. * '''1233''' (''[[Jōei]] 2''): [[Kujō Yoritsune]] completely rebuilt the structures of the shrine. In 1868, when [[Emperor Meiji]] made his first visit to [[Edo]], he dispatched an emissary to the shrine with a ''heikaku'' for the kami. In 1871, the shrine was listed under the name {{nihongo|'''Takebe Jinja'''|建部神社||}} as a prefectural shrine under the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines]] under [[State Shinto]]. In 1885, it was promoted to a {{nihongo|Imperial shrine, 2nd rank|官幣中社|[[Kanpei Chūsha]]}} and in 1900 to a {{nihongo|Imperial shrine, 1st rank|官幣大社|Kanpei-taisha}}.Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 124. In 1945, the shrine was used as the design for the first [[1000 yen note]] issued. In 1948 the shrine's name was officially changed to "Takebe Taisha".{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}} The shrine is 15 minutes on foot from [[Karahashimae Station]] on the [[Keihan Electric Railway]] [[Ishiyama Sakamoto Line]]. ==Gallery== Takebe Taisha1.jpg|First Torii Takebe Taisha honden.jpg|Honden Takebe Taisha homotsuden.jpg|Museum Takebe Taisha ishitoro.jpg|Stone lantern (ICP) Takebe Taisha2.jpg|Second Torii ==Cultural Properties== ===Important Cultural Properties=== *[[Tōrō]] stone lantern, dated 1270.{{cite web |url= https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/6834|title=石燈籠|trans-title=Ishi Tōrō|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} *Wooden Statue of a Female Deity (2 pcs), [[Heian period]].{{cite web |url= https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/3192|title=木造女神坐像|trans-title=Mokuzō nyoshinzazō|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} ==See also== *[[List of Shinto shrines]] *[[Ichinomiya]] ==References== {{reflist}} ==External links== {{commons category|Takebe Taisha}} *[http://takebetaisha.jp/ Official website] *[https://otsu.or.jp/en/thingstodo/spot49 Biwako-Otsu Travel Guide] {{Shinto shrine}} [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Kanpei Taisha]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Shiga Prefecture]] [[Category:Ichinomiya]] [[Category:Ōmi Province]] [[Category:Ōtsu]] [[Category:Myōjin Taisha]] [[Category:Taisha-zukuri]]

The shrine appears in various [[Heian period]] documents, including the ''[[Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku]]'', ''[[Ruijū Kokushi]]'' and the ''[[Engishiki]]'', and came to be regarded as the ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of Ōmi Province.{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=978-4569669304 |language=Japanese}} When [[Minamoto no Yoritomo]] was sent to exile in [[Izu Province|Izu]] following the defeat of the [[Genji clan]] in the [[Heiji Rebellion]], he stopped to pray for the revival of the Genji at this shrine. In 1190, when the [[Heike clan]] was overthrown, he again prayed at this shrine in thanksgiving, and the shrine became a popular pilgrimage destination for good fortune.Ponsonby-Farne, ''Visiting Shrines,'' p. 321. The shrine was rebuilt in 1233 by [[Kujō Yoritsune]]. * '''1233''' (''[[Jōei]] 2''): [[Kujō Yoritsune]] completely rebuilt the structures of the shrine. In 1868, when [[Emperor Meiji]] made his first visit to [[Edo]], he dispatched an emissary to the shrine with a ''heikaku'' for the kami. ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 124. In 1945, the shrine was used as the design for the first [[1000 yen note]] issued. In 1948 the shrine's name was officially changed to "Takebe Taisha".{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}} The shrine is 15 minutes on foot from [[Karahashimae Station]] on the [[Keihan Electric Railway]] [[Ishiyama Sakamoto Line]]. ==Gallery== Takebe Taisha1.jpg|First Torii Takebe Taisha honden.jpg|Honden Takebe Taisha homotsuden.jpg|Museum Takebe Taisha ishitoro.jpg|Stone lantern (ICP) Takebe Taisha2.jpg|Second Torii ==Cultural Properties== ===Important Cultural Properties=== *[[Tōrō]] stone lantern, dated 1270.{{cite web |url= https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/6834|title=石燈籠|trans-title=Ishi Tōrō|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} *Wooden Statue of a Female Deity (2 pcs), [[Heian period]].{{cite web |url= https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/3192|title=木造女神坐像|trans-title=Mokuzō nyoshinzazō|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} ==See also== *[[List of Shinto shrines]] *[[Ichinomiya]] ==References== {{reflist}} ==External links== {{commons category|Takebe Taisha}} *[http://takebetaisha.jp/ Official website] *[https://otsu.or.jp/en/thingstodo/spot49 Biwako-Otsu Travel Guide] {{Shinto shrine}} [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Kanpei Taisha]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Shiga Prefecture]] [[Category:Ichinomiya]] [[Category:Ōmi Province]] [[Category:Ōtsu]] [[Category:Myōjin Taisha]] [[Category:Taisha-zukuri]] [[Category:Yamato Takeru Legend]]

''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 124. In 1945, the shrine was used as the design for the first [[1000 yen note]] issued. In 1948 the shrine's name was officially changed to "Takebe Taisha".{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}} The shrine is 15 minutes on foot from [[Karahashimae Station]] on the [[Keihan Electric Railway]] [[Ishiyama Sakamoto Line]]. ==Gallery== Takebe Taisha1.jpg|First Torii Takebe Taisha honden.jpg|Honden Takebe Taisha homotsuden.jpg|Museum Takebe Taisha ishitoro.jpg|Stone lantern (ICP) Takebe Taisha2.jpg|Second Torii ==Cultural Properties== ===Important Cultural Properties=== *[[Tōrō]] stone lantern, dated 1270.{{cite web |url= https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/6834|title=石燈籠|trans-title=Ishi Tōrō|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} *Wooden Statue of a Female Deity (2 pcs), [[Heian period]].{{cite web |url= https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/3192|title=木造女神坐像|trans-title=Mokuzō nyoshinzazō|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} ==See also== *[[List of Shinto shrines]] *[[Ichinomiya]] ==References== {{reflist}} ==External links== {{commons category|Takebe Taisha}} *[http://takebetaisha.jp/ Official website] *[https://otsu.or.jp/en/thingstodo/spot49 Biwako-Otsu Travel Guide] {{Shinto shrine}} [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Kanpei Taisha]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Shiga Prefecture]] [[Category:Ichinomiya]] [[Category:Ōmi Province]] [[Category:Ōtsu]] [[Category:Myōjin Taisha]] [[Category:Taisha]] [[Category:Yamato Takeru Legend]] [[Category:Nagare-zukuri]]

revision.text:
{{Short description|Shinto shrine in Ōtsu, Shiga, Japan}} {{Use dmy dates|date=November 2019}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Takebe Taisha
建部大社 | image = Takebe-shrine.jpg | caption = ''Heiden'' of Takebe Taisha and three sacred sugi trees {{Infobox mapframe|tyep=shape|zoom=14|frame-width=240}} | map_type = Japan Shiga Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|34|58|24.6|N|135|54|48.62|E|type:landmark_region:JP|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = [[Yamato Takeru]]
[[Ōkuninushi|Ōkuninmushi no Mikoto]] | founded_by = [[Emperor Keiko]] | established = c.[[Kofun period]] | date_destroyed = | location = 16-1, Jinryō 1-chōme, Ōtsu, Shiga, Japan | website = {{Official|1=http://takebetaisha.jp/}} | architecture_style = ''[[Nagare-zukuri]]'' | festival = ''Reitaisai'' (15 April) | leadership = | footnotes ={{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=pink|text align=center|[[Important Cultural Properties of Japan|Important Cultural Property]]}} }} {{nihongo|'''Takebe Shrine'''|建部大社| Takebe Taisha}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in the city of [[Ōtsu, Shiga|Ōtsu]], [[Shiga Prefecture]], [[Japan]]. It is the ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of former [[Ōmi Province]].[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/images/uploads/EOS070712Ab.pdf "Nationwide List of ''Ichinomiya''," p. 1.]; retrieved 2011-08-010 The main ''[[kami]]'' enshrined are [[Ōkuninushi]] and [[Yamato Takeru]]. The shrine's main festival is held annually on April 15.{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=Shokoku jinja Ichinomiya Ninomiya San'nomiya |date=2015 |publisher= Yamakawa shuppansha |isbn=978-4634150867 |language=Japanese}} ==Enshrined ''kami''== The ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at Takebe Taisha are: * {{nihongo|'''[[Yamato Takeru]]'''| 日本武尊|}}, folk hero and the son of [[Emperor Keikō]] * {{nihongo|'''[[Ōkuninushi]]'''| 大己貴命|}}, the god of nation-building, agriculture, medicine, and protective magic ==History== According to the shrine's legend, its foundation has strong connections with the [[Yamato Takeru]] mythology. Following Yamato Takeru's death, his wife Princess Futaji resided at a place called Chigusatake, Takebe-go in what is now [[Kanzaki District, Shiga]], together with their son Takebe Inai Butsumei. A shrine was erected in that location 43rd year of the reign of the legendary [[Emperor Keikō]], who was Yamato Takeru's father. The place name "Takebe" is thought to come from the name "Takeru", and is also found in other places connected with theYamato Takeru myth. Later, under the reign of [[Emperor Tenmu]], the shrine was relocated to Seto, in [[Kurita District, Shiga|Kurita District]] and became the guardian shrine for Ōmi Province. However, in 755 the shrine was moved to its current location on orders of [[Empress Kōken]],Ponsonby-Fane, ''Visiting Shrines,'' p. 316. and [[Ōkuninushi]] was also installed as a deity at the shrine as a ''[[bunrei]]'' of [[Ōmiwa Shrine]]. The shrine appears in various [[Heian period]] documents, including the ''[[Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku]]'', ''[[Ruijū Kokushi]]'' and the ''[[Engishiki]]'', and came to be regarded as the ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of Ōmi Province.{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=978-4569669304 |language=Japanese}} When [[Minamoto no Yoritomo]] was sent to exile in [[Izu Province|Izu]] following the defeat of the [[Genji clan]] in the [[Heiji Rebellion]], he stopped to pray for the revival of the Genji at this shrine. In 1190, when the [[Heike clan]] was overthrown, he again prayed at this shrine in thanksgiving, and the shrine became a popular pilgrimage destination for good fortune.Ponsonby-Farne, ''Visiting Shrines,'' p. 321. The shrine was rebuilt in 1233 by [[Kujō Yoritsune]]. * '''1233''' (''[[Jōei]] 2''): [[Kujō Yoritsune]] completely rebuilt the structures of the shrine. In 1868, when [[Emperor Meiji]] made his first visit to [[Edo]], he dispatched an emissary to the shrine with a ''heikaku'' for the kami. In 1871, the shrine was listed under the name {{nihongo|'''Takebe Jinja'''|建部神社||}} as a prefectural shrine under the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines]] under [[State Shinto]]. In 1885, it was promoted to a {{nihongo|Imperial shrine, 2nd rank|官幣中社|[[Kanpei Chūsha]]}} and in 1900 to a {{nihongo|Imperial shrine, 1st rank|官幣大社|Kanpei-taisha}}.Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 124. In 1945, the shrine was used as the design for the first [[1000 yen note]] issued. In 1948 the shrine's name was officially changed to "Takebe Taisha".{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}} The shrine is 15 minutes on foot from [[Karahashimae Station]] on the [[Keihan Electric Railway]] [[Ishiyama Sakamoto Line]]. ==Gallery== Takebe Taisha1.jpg|First Torii Takebe Taisha honden.jpg|Honden Takebe Taisha homotsuden.jpg|Museum Takebe Taisha ishitoro.jpg|Stone lantern (ICP) Takebe Taisha2.jpg|Second Torii ==Cultural Properties== ===Important Cultural Properties=== *[[Tōrō]] stone lantern, dated 1270.{{cite web |url= https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/6834|title=石燈籠|trans-title=Ishi Tōrō|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} *Wooden Statue of a Female Deity (2 pcs), [[Heian period]].{{cite web |url= https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/3192|title=木造女神坐像|trans-title=Mokuzō nyoshinzazō|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} ==See also== *[[List of Shinto shrines]] *[[Ichinomiya]] ==References== {{reflist}} ==External links== {{commons category|Takebe Taisha}} *[http://takebetaisha.jp/ Official website] *[https://otsu.or.jp/en/thingstodo/spot49 Biwako-Otsu Travel Guide] {{Shinto shrine}} [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Kanpei Taisha]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Shiga Prefecture]] [[Category:Ichinomiya]] [[Category:Ōmi Province]] [[Category:Ōtsu]] [[Category:Myōjin Taisha]] [[Category:Taisha]] [[Category:Yamato Takeru Legend]] [[Category:Nagare-zukuri]]

However, in 755 the shrine was moved to its current location on orders of [[Empress Kōken]],Ponsonby-Fane, ''Visiting Shrines,'' p. 316. and [[Ōkuninushi]] was also installed as a deity at the shrine as a ''[[bunrei]]'' of [[Ōmiwa Shrine]]. The shrine appears in various [[Heian period]] documents, including the ''[[Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku]]'', ''[[Ruijū Kokushi]]'' and the ''[[Engishiki]]'', and came to be regarded as the ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of Ōmi Province.{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=978-4569669304 |language=Japanese}} When [[Minamoto no Yoritomo]] was sent to exile in [[Izu Province|Izu]] following the defeat of the [[Genji clan]] in the [[Heiji Rebellion]], he stopped to pray for the revival of the Genji at this shrine. In 1190, when the [[Heike clan]] was overthrown, he again prayed at this shrine in thanksgiving, and the shrine became a popular pilgrimage destination for good fortune.Ponsonby-Farne, ''Visiting Shrines,'' p. 321. The shrine was rebuilt in 1233 by [[Kujō Yoritsune]]. * '''1233''' (''[[Jōei]] 2''): [[Kujō Yoritsune]] completely rebuilt the structures of the shrine. In 1868, when [[Emperor Meiji]] made his first visit to [[Edo]], he dispatched an emissary to the shrine with a ''heikaku'' for the kami. In 1871, the shrine was listed under the name {{nihongo|'''Takebe Jinja'''|建部神社||}} as a prefectural shrine under the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines]] under [[State Shinto]]. In 1885, it was promoted to a {{nihongo|Imperial shrine, 2nd rank|官幣中社|[[Kanpei Chūsha]]}} and in 1900 to a {{nihongo|Imperial shrine, 1st rank|官幣大社|Kanpei-taisha}}.Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 124. In 1945, the shrine was used as the design for the first [[1000 yen note]] issued. In 1948 the shrine's name was officially changed to "Takebe Taisha".{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}} The shrine is 15 minutes on foot from [[Karahashimae Station]] on the [[Keihan Electric Railway]] [[Ishiyama Sakamoto Line]]. ==Gallery== Takebe Taisha1.jpg|First Torii Takebe Taisha honden.jpg|Honden Takebe Taisha homotsuden.jpg|Museum Takebe Taisha ishitoro.jpg|Stone lantern (ICP) Takebe Taisha2.jpg|Second Torii ==Cultural Properties== ===Important Cultural Properties=== *[[Tōrō]] stone lantern, dated 1270.{{cite web |url= https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/6834|title=石燈籠|trans-title=Ishi Tōrō|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} *Wooden Statue of a Female Deity (2 pcs), [[Heian period]].{{cite web |url= https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/3192|title=木造女神坐像|trans-title=Mokuzō nyoshinzazō|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} ==See also== *[[List of Shinto shrines]] *[[Ichinomiya]] ==References== {{reflist}} ==External links== {{commons category|Takebe Taisha}} *[http://takebetaisha.jp/ Official website] *[https://otsu.or.jp/en/thingstodo/spot49 Biwako-Otsu Travel Guide] {{Shinto shrine}} [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Kanpei Taisha]] [[en:Takebe taisha]][[Category:Shinto shrines in Shiga Prefecture]] [[Category:Ichinomiya]] [[Category:Ōmi Province]] [[Category:Ōtsu]] [[Category:Myōjin Taisha]] [[Category:Taisha]] [[Category:Yamato Takeru Legend]] [[Category:Nagare-zukuri]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia5]][[en:Takebe taisha]][[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia5]]

{{Short description|Shinto shrine in Ōtsu, Shiga, Japan}} {{Use dmy dates|date=November 2019}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Takebe Taisha
建部大社 | image = Takebe-shrine.jpg | caption = ''Heiden'' of Takebe Taisha and three sacred sugi trees {{Infobox mapframe|tyep=shape|zoom=14|frame-width=240}} | map_type = Japan Shiga Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|34|58|24.6|N|135|54|48.62|E|type:landmark_region:JP|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = [[Yamato Takeru]]
[[Ōkuninushi|Ōkuninmushi no Mikoto]] | founded_by = [[Emperor Keiko]] | established = c.[[Kofun period]] | date_destroyed = | location = 16-1, Jinryō 1-chōme, Ōtsu, Shiga, Japan | website = {{Official|1=http://takebetaisha.jp/}} | architecture_style = ''[[Nagare-zukuri]]'' | festival = ''Reitaisai'' (15 April) | leadership = | footnotes ={{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=pink|text align=center|[[Important Cultural Properties of Japan|Important Cultural Property]]}} }} {{nihongo|'''Takebe Shrine'''|建部大社| Takebe Taisha}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in the city of [[Ōtsu, Shiga|Ōtsu]], [[Shiga Prefecture]], [[Japan]]. It is the ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of former [[Ōmi Province]].[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/images/uploads/EOS070712Ab.pdf "Nationwide List of ''Ichinomiya''," p. 1.]; retrieved 2011-08-010 The main ''[[kami]]'' enshrined are [[Ōkuninushi]] and [[Yamato Takeru]]. The shrine's main festival is held annually on April 15.{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=Shokoku jinja Ichinomiya Ninomiya San'nomiya |date=2015 |publisher= Yamakawa shuppansha |isbn=978-4634150867 |language=Japanese}} ==Enshrined ''kami''== The ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at Takebe Taisha are: * {{nihongo|'''[[Yamato Takeru]]'''| 日本武尊|}}, folk hero and the son of [[Emperor Keikō]] * {{nihongo|'''[[Ōkuninushi]]'''| 大己貴命|}}, the god of nation-building, agriculture, medicine, and protective magic ==History== According to the shrine's legend, its foundation has strong connections with the [[Yamato Takeru]] mythology. Following Yamato Takeru's death, his wife Princess Futaji resided at a place called Chigusatake, Takebe-go in what is now [[Kanzaki District, Shiga]], together with their son Takebe Inai Butsumei. A shrine was erected in that location 43rd year of the reign of the legendary [[Emperor Keikō]], who was Yamato Takeru's father. The place name "Takebe" is thought to come from the name "Takeru", and is also found in other places connected with theYamato Takeru myth. Later, under the reign of [[Emperor Tenmu]], the shrine was relocated to Seto, in [[Kurita District, Shiga|Kurita District]] and became the guardian shrine for Ōmi Province. However, in 755 the shrine was moved to its current location on orders of [[Empress Kōken]],Ponsonby-Fane, ''Visiting Shrines,'' p. 316. and [[Ōkuninushi]] was also installed as a deity at the shrine as a ''[[bunrei]]'' of [[Ōmiwa Shrine]]. The shrine appears in various [[Heian period]] documents, including the ''[[Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku]]'', ''[[Ruijū Kokushi]]'' and the ''[[Engishiki]]'', and came to be regarded as the ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of Ōmi Province.{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=978-4569669304 |language=Japanese}} When [[Minamoto no Yoritomo]] was sent to exile in [[Izu Province|Izu]] following the defeat of the [[Genji clan]] in the [[Heiji Rebellion]], he stopped to pray for the revival of the Genji at this shrine. In 1190, when the [[Heike clan]] was overthrown, he again prayed at this shrine in thanksgiving, and the shrine became a popular pilgrimage destination for good fortune.Ponsonby-Farne, ''Visiting Shrines,'' p. 321. The shrine was rebuilt in 1233 by [[Kujō Yoritsune]]. * '''1233''' (''[[Jōei]] 2''): [[Kujō Yoritsune]] completely rebuilt the structures of the shrine. In 1868, when [[Emperor Meiji]] made his first visit to [[Edo]], he dispatched an emissary to the shrine with a ''heikaku'' for the kami. In 1871, the shrine was listed under the name {{nihongo|'''Takebe Jinja'''|建部神社||}} as a prefectural shrine under the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines]] under [[State Shinto]]. In 1885, it was promoted to a {{nihongo|Imperial shrine, 2nd rank|官幣中社|[[Kanpei Chūsha]]}} and in 1900 to a {{nihongo|Imperial shrine, 1st rank|官幣大社|Kanpei-taisha}}.Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 124. In 1945, the shrine was used as the design for the first [[1000 yen note]] issued. In 1948 the shrine's name was officially changed to "Takebe Taisha".{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}} The shrine is 15 minutes on foot from [[Karahashimae Station]] on the [[Keihan Electric Railway]] [[Ishiyama Sakamoto Line]]. ==Gallery== Takebe Taisha1.jpg|First Torii Takebe Taisha honden.jpg|Honden Takebe Taisha homotsuden.jpg|Museum Takebe Taisha ishitoro.jpg|Stone lantern (ICP) Takebe Taisha2.jpg|Second Torii ==Cultural Properties== ===Important Cultural Properties=== *[[Tōrō]] stone lantern, dated 1270.{{cite web |url= https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/6834|title=石燈籠|trans-title=Ishi Tōrō|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} *Wooden Statue of a Female Deity (2 pcs), [[Heian period]].{{cite web |url= https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/3192|title=木造女神坐像|trans-title=Mokuzō nyoshinzazō|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} ==See also== *[[List of Shinto shrines]] *[[Ichinomiya]] ==References== {{reflist}} ==External links== {{commons category|Takebe Taisha}} *[http://takebetaisha.jp/ Official website] *[https://otsu.or.jp/en/thingstodo/spot49 Biwako-Otsu Travel Guide] {{Shinto shrine}} [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Kanpei Taisha]] [[en:Takebe taisha]][[Category:Shinto shrines in Shiga Prefecture]] [[Category:Ichinomiya]] [[Category:Ōmi Province]] [[Category:Ōtsu]] [[Category:Myōjin Taisha]] [[Category:Taisha]] [[Category:Yamato Takeru Legend]] [[Category:Nagare-zukuri]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia5]][[en:Takebe taisha]][[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia5]] [[Category:Pages from wikipedia]]
