{{Short description|Shinto shrine in Ōtsu, Shiga, Japan}} {{Use dmy dates|date=November 2019}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Takebe Taisha
建部大社 | image = Takebe-shrine.jpg | caption = ''Heiden'' of Takebe Taisha and three sacred sugi trees {{Infobox mapframe|tyep=shape|zoom=14|frame-width=240}} | map_type = Japan Shiga Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|34|58|24.6|N|135|54|48.62|E|type:landmark_region:JP|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = [[Yamato Takeru]]
[[Ōkuninushi|Ōkuninmushi no Mikoto]] | founded_by = [[Emperor Keiko]] | established = c.[[Kofun period]] | date_destroyed = | location = 16-1, Jinryō 1-chōme, Ōtsu, Shiga, Japan | website = {{Official|1=http://takebetaisha.jp/}} | architecture_style = ''[[Nagare-zukuri]]'' | festival = ''Reitaisai'' (15 April) | leadership = | footnotes ={{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=pink|text align=center|[[Important Cultural Properties of Japan|Important Cultural Property]]}} }} {{nihongo|'''Takebe Shrine'''|建部大社| Takebe Taisha}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in the city of [[Ōtsu, Shiga|Ōtsu]], [[Shiga Prefecture]], [[Japan]]. It is the ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of former [[Ōmi Province]].[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/images/uploads/EOS070712Ab.pdf "Nationwide List of ''Ichinomiya''," p. 1.]; retrieved 2011-08-010 The main ''[[kami]]'' enshrined are [[Ōkuninushi]] and [[Yamato Takeru]]. The shrine's main festival is held annually on April 15.{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=Shokoku jinja Ichinomiya Ninomiya San'nomiya |date=2015 |publisher= Yamakawa shuppansha |isbn=978-4634150867 |language=Japanese}} ==Enshrined ''kami''== The ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at Takebe Taisha are: * {{nihongo|'''[[Yamato Takeru]]'''| 日本武尊|}}, folk hero and the son of [[Emperor Keikō]] * {{nihongo|'''[[Ōkuninushi]]'''| 大己貴命|}}, the god of nation-building, agriculture, medicine, and protective magic ==History== According to the shrine's legend, its foundation has strong connections with the [[Yamato Takeru]] mythology. Following Yamato Takeru's death, his wife Princess Futaji resided at a place called Chigusatake, Takebe-go in what is now [[Kanzaki District, Shiga]], together with their son Takebe Inai Butsumei. A shrine was erected in that location 43rd year of the reign of the legendary [[Emperor Keikō]], who was Yamato Takeru's father. The place name "Takebe" is thought to come from the name "Takeru", and is also found in other places connected with theYamato Takeru myth. Later, under the reign of [[Emperor Tenmu]], the shrine was relocated to Seto, in [[Kurita District, Shiga|Kurita District]] and became the guardian shrine for Ōmi Province. However, in 755 the shrine was moved to its current location on orders of [[Empress Kōken]],Ponsonby-Fane, ''Visiting Shrines,'' p. 316. and [[Ōkuninushi]] was also installed as a deity at the shrine as a ''[[bunrei]]'' of [[Ōmiwa Shrine]]. The shrine appears in various [[Heian period]] documents, including the ''[[Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku]]'', ''[[Ruijū Kokushi]]'' and the ''[[Engishiki]]'', and came to be regarded as the ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of Ōmi Province.{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=978-4569669304 |language=Japanese}} When [[Minamoto no Yoritomo]] was sent to exile in [[Izu Province|Izu]] following the defeat of the [[Genji clan]] in the [[Heiji Rebellion]], he stopped to pray for the revival of the Genji at this shrine. In 1190, when the [[Heike clan]] was overthrown, he again prayed at this shrine in thanksgiving, and the shrine became a popular pilgrimage destination for good fortune.Ponsonby-Farne, ''Visiting Shrines,'' p. 321. The shrine was rebuilt in 1233 by [[Kujō Yoritsune]]. * '''1233''' (''[[Jōei]] 2''): [[Kujō Yoritsune]] completely rebuilt the structures of the shrine. In 1868, when [[Emperor Meiji]] made his first visit to [[Edo]], he dispatched an emissary to the shrine with a ''heikaku'' for the kami. In 1871, the shrine was listed under the name {{nihongo|'''Takebe Jinja'''|建部神社||}} as a prefectural shrine under the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines]] under [[State Shinto]]. In 1885, it was promoted to a {{nihongo|Imperial shrine, 2nd rank|官幣中社|[[Kanpei Chūsha]]}} and in 1900 to a {{nihongo|Imperial shrine, 1st rank|官幣大社|Kanpei-taisha}}.Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 124. In 1945, the shrine was used as the design for the first [[1000 yen note]] issued. In 1948 the shrine's name was officially changed to "Takebe Taisha".{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}} The shrine is 15 minutes on foot from [[Karahashimae Station]] on the [[Keihan Electric Railway]] [[Ishiyama Sakamoto Line]]. ==Gallery== Takebe Taisha1.jpg|First Torii Takebe Taisha honden.jpg|Honden Takebe Taisha homotsuden.jpg|Museum Takebe Taisha ishitoro.jpg|Stone lantern (ICP) Takebe Taisha2.jpg|Second Torii ==Cultural Properties== ===Important Cultural Properties=== *[[Tōrō]] stone lantern, dated 1270.{{cite web |url= https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/6834|title=石燈籠|trans-title=Ishi Tōrō|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} *Wooden Statue of a Female Deity (2 pcs), [[Heian period]].{{cite web |url= https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/3192|title=木造女神坐像|trans-title=Mokuzō nyoshinzazō|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} ==See also== *[[List of Shinto shrines]] *[[Ichinomiya]] ==References== {{reflist}} ==External links== {{commons category|Takebe Taisha}} *[http://takebetaisha.jp/ Official website] *[https://otsu.or.jp/en/thingstodo/spot49 Biwako-Otsu Travel Guide] {{Shinto shrine}} [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Kanpei Taisha]] [[en:Takebe taisha]][[Category:Shinto shrines in Shiga Prefecture]] [[Category:Ichinomiya]] [[Category:Ōmi Province]] [[Category:Ōtsu]] [[Category:Myōjin Taisha]] [[Category:Taisha]] [[Category:Yamato Takeru Legend]] [[Category:Nagare-zukuri]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia30]] [[en:Takebe taisha]][[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia30]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia23]] [[Category:dd]]

''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 124. In 1945, the shrine was used as the design for the first [[1000 yen note]] issued. In 1948 the shrine's name was officially changed to "Takebe Taisha".{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}} The shrine is 15 minutes on foot from [[Karahashimae Station]] on the [[Keihan Electric Railway]] [[Ishiyama Sakamoto Line]]. ==Gallery== Takebe Taisha1.jpg|First Torii Takebe Taisha honden.jpg|Honden Takebe Taisha homotsuden.jpg|Museum Takebe Taisha ishitoro.jpg|Stone lantern (ICP) Takebe Taisha2.jpg|Second Torii ==Cultural Properties== ===Important Cultural Properties=== *[[Tōrō]] stone lantern, dated 1270.{{cite web |url= https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/6834|title=石燈籠|trans-title=Ishi Tōrō|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} *Wooden Statue of a Female Deity (2 pcs), [[Heian period]].{{cite web |url= https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/3192|title=木造女神坐像|trans-title=Mokuzō nyoshinzazō|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} ==See also== *[[List of Shinto shrines]] *[[Ichinomiya]] ==References== {{reflist}} ==External links== {{commons category|Takebe Taisha}} *[http://takebetaisha.jp/ Official website] *[https://otsu.or.jp/en/thingstodo/spot49 Biwako-Otsu Travel Guide] {{Shinto shrine}} [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Kanpei Taisha]] [[en:Takebe taisha]][[Category:Shinto shrines in Shiga Prefecture]] [[Category:Ichinomiya]] [[Category:Ōmi Province]] [[Category:Ōtsu]] [[Category:Myōjin Taisha]] [[Category:Taisha]] [[Category:Yamato Takeru Legend]] [[Category:Nagare-zukuri]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia05]] [[en:Takebe taisha]][[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia05]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia23]] [[Category:dd]]

{{Short description|Shinto shrine in Ōtsu, Shiga, Japan}} {{Use dmy dates|date=November 2019}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Takebe Taisha
建部大社 | image = Takebe-shrine.jpg | caption = ''Heiden'' of Takebe Taisha and three sacred sugi trees {{Infobox mapframe|tyep=shape|zoom=14|frame-width=240}} | map_type = Japan Shiga Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|34|58|24.6|N|135|54|48.62|E|type:landmark_region:JP|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = [[Yamato Takeru]]
[[Ōkuninushi|Ōkuninmushi no Mikoto]] | founded_by = [[Emperor Keiko]] | established = c.[[Kofun period]] | date_destroyed = | location = 16-1, Jinryō 1-chōme, Ōtsu, Shiga, Japan | website = {{Official|1=http://takebetaisha.jp/}} | architecture_style = ''[[Nagare-zukuri]]'' | festival = ''Reitaisai'' (15 April) | leadership = | footnotes ={{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=pink|text align=center|[[Important Cultural Properties of Japan|Important Cultural Property]]}} }} {{nihongo|'''Takebe Shrine'''|建部大社| Takebe Taisha}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in the city of [[Ōtsu, Shiga|Ōtsu]], [[Shiga Prefecture]], [[Japan]]. It is the ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of former [[Ōmi Province]].[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/images/uploads/EOS070712Ab.pdf "Nationwide List of ''Ichinomiya''," p. 1.]; retrieved 2011-08-010 The main ''[[kami]]'' enshrined are [[Ōkuninushi]] and [[Yamato Takeru]]. The shrine's main festival is held annually on April 15.{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=Shokoku jinja Ichinomiya Ninomiya San'nomiya |date=2015 |publisher= Yamakawa shuppansha |isbn=978-4634150867 |language=Japanese}} ==Enshrined ''kami''== The ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at Takebe Taisha are: * {{nihongo|'''[[Yamato Takeru]]'''| 日本武尊|}}, folk hero and the son of [[Emperor Keikō]] * {{nihongo|'''[[Ōkuninushi]]'''| 大己貴命|}}, the god of nation-building, agriculture, medicine, and protective magic ==History== According to the shrine's legend, its foundation has strong connections with the [[Yamato Takeru]] mythology. Following Yamato Takeru's death, his wife Princess Futaji resided at a place called Chigusatake, Takebe-go in what is now [[Kanzaki District, Shiga]], together with their son Takebe Inai Butsumei. A shrine was erected in that location 43rd year of the reign of the legendary [[Emperor Keikō]], who was Yamato Takeru's father. The place name "Takebe" is thought to come from the name "Takeru", and is also found in other places connected with theYamato Takeru myth. Later, under the reign of [[Emperor Tenmu]], the shrine was relocated to Seto, in [[Kurita District, Shiga|Kurita District]] and became the guardian shrine for Ōmi Province. However, in 755 the shrine was moved to its current location on orders of [[Empress Kōken]],Ponsonby-Fane, ''Visiting Shrines,'' p. 316. and [[Ōkuninushi]] was also installed as a deity at the shrine as a ''[[bunrei]]'' of [[Ōmiwa Shrine]]. The shrine appears in various [[Heian period]] documents, including the ''[[Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku]]'', ''[[Ruijū Kokushi]]'' and the ''[[Engishiki]]'', and came to be regarded as the ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of Ōmi Province.{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=978-4569669304 |language=Japanese}} When [[Minamoto no Yoritomo]] was sent to exile in [[Izu Province|Izu]] following the defeat of the [[Genji clan]] in the [[Heiji Rebellion]], he stopped to pray for the revival of the Genji at this shrine. In 1190, when the [[Heike clan]] was overthrown, he again prayed at this shrine in thanksgiving, and the shrine became a popular pilgrimage destination for good fortune.Ponsonby-Farne, ''Visiting Shrines,'' p. 321. The shrine was rebuilt in 1233 by [[Kujō Yoritsune]]. * '''1233''' (''[[Jōei]] 2''): [[Kujō Yoritsune]] completely rebuilt the structures of the shrine. In 1868, when [[Emperor Meiji]] made his first visit to [[Edo]], he dispatched an emissary to the shrine with a ''heikaku'' for the kami. In 1871, the shrine was listed under the name {{nihongo|'''Takebe Jinja'''|建部神社||}} as a prefectural shrine under the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines]] under [[State Shinto]]. In 1885, it was promoted to a {{nihongo|Imperial shrine, 2nd rank|官幣中社|[[Kanpei Chūsha]]}} and in 1900 to a {{nihongo|Imperial shrine, 1st rank|官幣大社|Kanpei-taisha}}.Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 124. In 1945, the shrine was used as the design for the first [[1000 yen note]] issued. In 1948 the shrine's name was officially changed to "Takebe Taisha".{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}} The shrine is 15 minutes on foot from [[Karahashimae Station]] on the [[Keihan Electric Railway]] [[Ishiyama Sakamoto Line]]. ==Gallery== Takebe Taisha1.jpg|First Torii Takebe Taisha honden.jpg|Honden Takebe Taisha homotsuden.jpg|Museum Takebe Taisha ishitoro.jpg|Stone lantern (ICP) Takebe Taisha2.jpg|Second Torii ==Cultural Properties== ===Important Cultural Properties=== *[[Tōrō]] stone lantern, dated 1270.{{cite web |url= https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/6834|title=石燈籠|trans-title=Ishi Tōrō|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} *Wooden Statue of a Female Deity (2 pcs), [[Heian period]].{{cite web |url= https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/3192|title=木造女神坐像|trans-title=Mokuzō nyoshinzazō|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} ==See also== *[[List of Shinto shrines]] *[[Ichinomiya]] ==References== {{reflist}} ==External links== {{commons category|Takebe Taisha}} *[http://takebetaisha.jp/ Official website] *[https://otsu.or.jp/en/thingstodo/spot49 Biwako-Otsu Travel Guide] {{Shinto shrine}} [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Kanpei Taisha]] [[en:Takebe taisha]][[Category:Shinto shrines in Shiga Prefecture]] [[Category:Ichinomiya]] [[Category:Ōmi Province]] [[Category:Ōtsu]] [[Category:Myōjin Taisha]] [[Category:Taisha]] [[Category:Yamato Takeru Legend]] [[Category:Nagare-zukuri]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia]] [[en:Takebe taisha]][[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia]] [[Category:dd]]

The shrine was rebuilt in 1233 by [[Kujō Yoritsune]]. * '''1233''' (''[[Jōei]] 2''): [[Kujō Yoritsune]] completely rebuilt the structures of the shrine. In 1868, when [[Emperor Meiji]] made his first visit to [[Edo]], he dispatched an emissary to the shrine with a ''heikaku'' for the kami. {{Short description|Shinto shrine in Ōtsu, Shiga, Japan}} {{Use dmy dates|date=November 2019}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Takebe Taisha
建部大社 | image = Takebe-shrine.jpg | caption = ''Heiden'' of Takebe Taisha and three sacred sugi trees {{Infobox mapframe|tyep=shape|zoom=14|frame-width=240}} | map_type = Japan Shiga Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|34|58|24.6|N|135|54|48.62|E|type:landmark_region:JP|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = [[Yamato Takeru]]
[[Ōkuninushi|Ōkuninmushi no Mikoto]] | founded_by = [[Emperor Keiko]] | established = c.[[Kofun period]] | date_destroyed = | location = 16-1, Jinryō 1-chōme, Ōtsu, Shiga, Japan | website = {{Official|1=http://takebetaisha.jp/}} | architecture_style = ''[[Nagare-zukuri]]'' | festival = ''Reitaisai'' (15 April) | leadership = | footnotes ={{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=pink|text align=center|[[Important Cultural Properties of Japan|Important Cultural Property]]}} }} {{nihongo|'''Takebe Shrine'''|建部大社| Takebe Taisha}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in the city of [[Ōtsu, Shiga|Ōtsu]], [[Shiga Prefecture]], [[Japan]]. It is the ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of former [[Ōmi Province]].[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/images/uploads/EOS070712Ab.pdf "Nationwide List of ''Ichinomiya''," p. 1.]; retrieved 2011-08-010 The main ''[[kami]]'' enshrined are [[Ōkuninushi]] and [[Yamato Takeru]]. The shrine's main festival is held annually on April 15.{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=Shokoku jinja Ichinomiya Ninomiya San'nomiya |date=2015 |publisher= Yamakawa shuppansha |isbn=978-4634150867 |language=Japanese}} ==Enshrined ''kami''== The ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at Takebe Taisha are: * {{nihongo|'''[[Yamato Takeru]]'''| 日本武尊|}}, folk hero and the son of [[Emperor Keikō]] * {{nihongo|'''[[Ōkuninushi]]'''| 大己貴命|}}, the god of nation-building, agriculture, medicine, and protective magic ==History== According to the shrine's legend, its foundation has strong connections with the [[Yamato Takeru]] mythology. Following Yamato Takeru's death, his wife Princess Futaji resided at a place called Chigusatake, Takebe-go in what is now [[Kanzaki District, Shiga]], together with their son Takebe Inai Butsumei. A shrine was erected in that location 43rd year of the reign of the legendary [[Emperor Keikō]], who was Yamato Takeru's father. The place name "Takebe" is thought to come from the name "Takeru", and is also found in other places connected with theYamato Takeru myth. Later, under the reign of [[Emperor Tenmu]], the shrine was relocated to Seto, in [[Kurita District, Shiga|Kurita District]] and became the guardian shrine for Ōmi Province. However, in 755 the shrine was moved to its current location on orders of [[Empress Kōken]],Ponsonby-Fane, ''Visiting Shrines,'' p. 316. and [[Ōkuninushi]] was also installed as a deity at the shrine as a ''[[bunrei]]'' of [[Ōmiwa Shrine]]. The shrine appears in various [[Heian period]] documents, including the ''[[Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku]]'', ''[[Ruijū Kokushi]]'' and the ''[[Engishiki]]'', and came to be regarded as the ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of Ōmi Province.{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=978-4569669304 |language=Japanese}} When [[Minamoto no Yoritomo]] was sent to exile in [[Izu Province|Izu]] following the defeat of the [[Genji clan]] in the [[Heiji Rebellion]], he stopped to pray for the revival of the Genji at this shrine. In 1190, when the [[Heike clan]] was overthrown, he again prayed at this shrine in thanksgiving, and the shrine became a popular pilgrimage destination for good fortune.Ponsonby-Farne, ''Visiting Shrines,'' p. 321. The shrine was rebuilt in 1233 by [[Kujō Yoritsune]]. * '''1233''' (''[[Jōei]] 2''): [[Kujō Yoritsune]] completely rebuilt the structures of the shrine. In 1868, when [[Emperor Meiji]] made his first visit to [[Edo]], he dispatched an emissary to the shrine with a ''heikaku'' for the kami. In 1871, the shrine was listed under the name {{nihongo|'''Takebe Jinja'''|建部神社||}} as a prefectural shrine under the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines]] under [[State Shinto]]. In 1885, it was promoted to a {{nihongo|Imperial shrine, 2nd rank|官幣中社|[[Kanpei Chūsha]]}} and in 1900 to a {{nihongo|Imperial shrine, 1st rank|官幣大社|Kanpei-taisha}}.Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 124. In 1945, the shrine was used as the design for the first [[1000 yen note]] issued. In 1948 the shrine's name was officially changed to "Takebe Taisha".{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}} The shrine is 15 minutes on foot from [[Karahashimae Station]] on the [[Keihan Electric Railway]] [[Ishiyama Sakamoto Line]]. ==Gallery== Takebe Taisha1.jpg|First Torii Takebe Taisha honden.jpg|Honden Takebe Taisha homotsuden.jpg|Museum Takebe Taisha ishitoro.jpg|Stone lantern (ICP) Takebe Taisha2.jpg|Second Torii ==Cultural Properties== ===Important Cultural Properties=== *[[Tōrō]] stone lantern, dated 1270.{{cite web |url= https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/6834|title=石燈籠|trans-title=Ishi Tōrō|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} *Wooden Statue of a Female deity (2 pcs), [[Heian period]].{{cite web |url= https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/3192|title=木造女神坐像|trans-title=Mokuzō nyoshinzazō|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} ==See also== *[[List of Shinto shrines]] *[[Ichinomiya]] ==References== {{reflist}} ==External links== {{commons category|Takebe Taisha}} *[http://takebetaisha.jp/ Official website] *[https://otsu.or.jp/en/thingstodo/spot49 Biwako-Otsu Travel Guide] {{Shinto shrine}} [[category:kk]][[category:Beppyo shrines]] [[category:kk]][[category:Kanpei Taisha]] [[en:Takebe taisha]][[category:kk]][[category:Shinto shrines in Shiga Prefecture]] [[category:kk]][[category:Ichinomiya]] [[category:kk]][[category:Ōmi Province]] [[category:kk]][[category:Ōtsu]] [[category:kk]][[category:Myōjin Taisha]] [[category:kk]][[category:Taisha]] [[category:kk]][[category:Yamato Takeru Legend]] [[category:kk]][[category:Nagare-zukuri]] [[category:kk]][[category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia]][[en:{{subst:PAGENAME]]]] [[category:mm]] [[en:Takebe taisha]][[category:kk]][[category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia]][[en:{{subst:PAGENAME]]]] [[category:mm]] However, in 755 the shrine was moved to its current location on orders of [[Empress Kōken]],Ponsonby-Fane, ''Visiting Shrines,'' p. 316. and [[Ōkuninushi]] was also installed as a deity at the shrine as a ''[[bunrei]]'' of [[Ōmiwa Shrine]]. The shrine appears in various [[Heian period]] documents, including the ''[[Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku]]'', ''[[Ruijū Kokushi]]'' and the ''[[Engishiki]]'', and came to be regarded as the ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of Ōmi Province.{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=978-4569669304 |language=Japanese}} When [[Minamoto no Yoritomo]] was sent to exile in [[Izu Province|Izu]] following the defeat of the [[Genji clan]] in the [[Heiji Rebellion]], he stopped to pray for the revival of the Genji at this shrine. In 1190, when the [[Heike clan]] was overthrown, he again prayed at this shrine in thanksgiving, and the shrine became a popular pilgrimage destination for good fortune.Ponsonby-Farne, ''Visiting Shrines,'' p. 321. The shrine was rebuilt in 1233 by [[Kujō Yoritsune]]. * '''1233''' (''[[Jōei]] 2''): [[Kujō Yoritsune]] completely rebuilt the structures of the shrine. In 1868, when [[Emperor Meiji]] made his first visit to [[Edo]], he dispatched an emissary to the shrine with a ''heikaku'' for the kami. In 1868, when [[Emperor Meiji]] made his first visit to [[Edo]], he dispatched an emissary to the shrine with a ''heikaku'' for the kami. It is the ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of former [[Ōmi Province]].[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/images/uploads/EOS070712Ab.pdf "Nationwide List of ''Ichinomiya''," p. 1.]; retrieved 2011-08-010 The main ''[[kami]]'' enshrined are [[Ōkuninushi]] and [[Yamato Takeru]]. Following Yamato Takeru's death, his wife Princess Futaji resided at a place called Chigusatake, Takebe-go in what is now [[Kanzaki District, Shiga]], together with their son Takebe Inai Butsumei. The place name "Takebe" is thought to come from the name "Takeru", and is also found in other places connected with theYamato Takeru myth. Later, under the reign of [[Emperor Tenmu]], the shrine was relocated to Seto, in [[Kurita District, Shiga|Kurita District]] and became the guardian shrine for Ōmi Province. The shrine appears in various [[Heian period]] documents, including the ''[[Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku]]'', ''[[Ruijū Kokushi]]'' and the ''[[Engishiki]]'', and came to be regarded as the ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of Ōmi Province.{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=978-4569669304 |language=Japanese}} When [[Minamoto no Yoritomo]] was sent to exile in [[Izu Province|Izu]] following the defeat of the [[Genji clan]] in the [[Heiji Rebellion]], he stopped to pray for the revival of the Genji at this shrine. In 1190, when the [[Heike clan]] was overthrown, he again prayed at this shrine in thanksgiving, and the shrine became a popular pilgrimage destination for good fortune.Ponsonby-Farne, ''Visiting Shrines,'' p. 321. The shrine was rebuilt in 1233 by [[Kujō Yoritsune]]. * '''1233''' (''[[Jōei]] 2''): [[Kujō Yoritsune]] completely rebuilt the structures of the shrine. In 1868, when [[Emperor Meiji]] made his first visit to [[Edo]], he dispatched an emissary to the shrine with a ''heikaku'' for the kami. In 1871, the shrine was listed under the name {{nihongo|'''Takebe Jinja'''|建部神社||}} as a prefectural shrine under the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines]] under [[State Shinto]]. In 1885, it was promoted to a {{nihongo|Imperial shrine, 2nd rank|官幣中社|[[Kanpei Chūsha]]}} and in 1900 to a {{nihongo|Imperial shrine, 1st rank|官幣大社|Kanpei-taisha}}.Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 124. In 1945, the shrine was used as the design for the first [[1000 yen note]] issued. In 1948 the shrine's name was officially changed to "Takebe Taisha".{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}} The shrine is 15 minutes on foot from [[Karahashimae Station]] on the [[Keihan Electric Railway]] [[Ishiyama Sakamoto Line]]. ==Gallery== Takebe and Categories from wikipedia]][[en:Takebe taisha]]]] [[category:mm]] [[en:Takebe taisha]][[category:kk]][[category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia]][[en:Takebe taisha]]]] [[category:mm]] {{Short description|Shinto shrine in Ōtsu, Shiga, Japan}} {{Use dmy dates|date=November 2019}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Takebe Taisha
建部大社 | image = Takebe-shrine.jpg | caption = ''Heiden'' of Takebe Taisha and three sacred sugi trees {{Infobox mapframe|tyep=shape|zoom=14|frame-width=240}} | map_type = Japan Shiga Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|34|58|24.6|N|135|54|48.62|E|type:landmark_region:JP|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = [[Yamato Takeru]]
[[Ōkuninushi|Ōkuninmushi no Mikoto]] | founded_by = [[Emperor Keiko]] | established = c.[[Kofun period]] | date_destroyed = | location = 16-1, Jinryō 1-chōme, Ōtsu, Shiga, Japan | website = {{Official|1=http://takebetaisha.jp/}} | architecture_style = ''[[Nagare-zukuri]]'' | festival = ''Reitaisai'' (15 April) | leadership = | footnotes ={{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=pink|text align=center|[[Important Cultural Properties of Japan|Important Cultural Property]]}} }} {{nihongo|'''Takebe Shrine'''|建部大社| Takebe Taisha}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in the city of [[Ōtsu, Shiga|Ōtsu]], [[Shiga Prefecture]], [[Japan]]. It is the ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of former [[Ōmi Province]].[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/images/uploads/EOS070712Ab.pdf "Nationwide List of ''Ichinomiya''," p. 1.]; retrieved 2011-08-010 The main ''[[kami]]'' enshrined are [[Ōkuninushi]] and [[Yamato Takeru]]. The shrine's main festival is held annually on April 15.{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=Shokoku jinja Ichinomiya Ninomiya San'nomiya |date=2015 |publisher= Yamakawa shuppansha |isbn=978-4634150867 |language=Japanese}} ==Enshrined ''kami''== The ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at Takebe Taisha are: * {{nihongo|'''[[Yamato Takeru]]'''| 日本武尊|}}, folk hero and the son of [[Emperor Keikō]] * {{nihongo|'''[[Ōkuninushi]]'''| 大己貴命|}}, the god of nation-building, agriculture, medicine, and protective magic ==History== According to the shrine's legend, its foundation has strong connections with the [[Yamato Takeru]] mythology. Following Yamato Takeru's death, his wife Princess Futaji resided at a place called Chigusatake, Takebe-go in what is now [[Kanzaki District, Shiga]], together with their son Takebe Inai Butsumei. A shrine was erected in that location 43rd year of the reign of the legendary [[Emperor Keikō]], who was Yamato Takeru's father. The place name "Takebe" is thought to come from the name "Takeru", and is also found in other places connected with theYamato Takeru myth. Later, under the reign of [[Emperor Tenmu]], the shrine was relocated to Seto, in [[Kurita District, Shiga|Kurita District]] and became the guardian shrine for Ōmi Province. However, in 755 the shrine was moved to its current location on orders of [[Empress Kōken]],Ponsonby-Fane, ''Visiting Shrines,'' p. 316. and [[Ōkuninushi]] was also installed as a deity at the shrine as a ''[[bunrei]]'' of [[Ōmiwa Shrine]]. The shrine appears in various [[Heian period]] documents, including the ''[[Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku]]'', ''[[Ruijū Kokushi]]'' and the ''[[Engishiki]]'', and came to be regarded as the ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of Ōmi Province.{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=978-4569669304 |language=Japanese}} When [[Minamoto no Yoritomo]] was sent to exile in [[Izu Province|Izu]] following the defeat of the [[Genji clan]] in the [[Heiji Rebellion]], he stopped to pray for the revival of the Genji at this shrine. In 1190, when the [[Heike clan]] was overthrown, he again prayed at this shrine in thanksgiving, and the shrine became a popular pilgrimage destination for good fortune.Ponsonby-Farne, ''Visiting Shrines,'' p. 321. The shrine was rebuilt in 1233 by [[Kujō Yoritsune]]. * '''1233''' (''[[Jōei]] 2''): [[Kujō Yoritsune]] completely rebuilt the structures of the shrine. In 1868, when [[Emperor Meiji]] made his first visit to [[Edo]], he dispatched an emissary to the shrine with a ''heikaku'' for the kami. In 1871, the shrine was listed under the name {{nihongo|'''Takebe Jinja'''|建部神社||}} as a prefectural shrine under the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines]] under [[State Shinto]]. In 1885, it was promoted to a {{nihongo|Imperial shrine, 2nd rank|官幣中社|[[Kanpei Chūsha]]}} and in 1900 to a {{nihongo|Imperial shrine, 1st rank|官幣大社|Kanpei-taisha}}.Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 124. In 1945, the shrine was used as the design for the first [[1000 yen note]] issued. In 1948 the shrine's name was officially changed to "Takebe Taisha".{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}} The shrine is 15 minutes on foot from [[Karahashimae Station]] on the [[Keihan Electric Railway]] [[Ishiyama Sakamoto Line]]. ==Gallery== Takebe Taisha1.jpg|First Torii Takebe Taisha honden.jpg|Honden Takebe Taisha homotsuden.jpg|Museum Takebe Taisha ishitoro.jpg|Stone lantern (ICP) Takebe Taisha2.jpg|Second Torii ==Cultural Properties== ===Important Cultural Properties=== *[[Tōrō]] stone lantern, dated 1270.{{cite web |url= https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/6834|title=石燈籠|trans-title=Ishi Tōrō|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} *Wooden Statue of a Female deity (2 pcs), [[Heian period]].{{cite web |url= https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/3192|title=木造女神坐像|trans-title=Mokuzō nyoshinzazō|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} ==See also== *[[List of Shinto shrines]] *[[Ichinomiya]] ==References== {{reflist}} ==External links== {{commons category|Takebe Taisha}} *[http://takebetaisha.jp/ Official website] *[https://otsu.or.jp/en/thingstodo/spot49 Biwako-Otsu Travel Guide] {{Shinto shrine}} [[category:kk]][[category:Beppyo shrines]] [[category:kk]][[category:Kanpei Taisha]] [[en:Takebe taisha]][[category:kk]][[category:Shinto shrines in Shiga Prefecture]] [[category:kk]][[category:Ichinomiya]] [[category:kk]][[category:Ōmi Province]] [[category:kk]][[category:Ōtsu]] [[category:kk]][[category:Myōjin Taisha]] [[category:kk]][[category:Taisha]] [[category:kk]][[category:Yamato Takeru Legend]] [[category:kk]][[category:Nagare-zukuri]] [[category:kk]][[category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia]][[en:Takebe taisha]]]] [[category:mm]] [[en:Takebe taisha]][[category:kk]][[category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia]][[en:Takebe taisha]]]] [[category:mm]] Following Yamato Takeru's death, his wife Princess Futaji resided at a place called Chigusatake, Takebe-go in what is now [[Kanzaki District, Shiga]], together with their son Takebe Inai Butsumei. A shrine was erected in that location 43rd year of the reign of the legendary [[Emperor Keikō]], who was Yamato Takeru's father. The place name "Takebe" is thought to come from the name "Takeru", and is also found in other places connected with theYamato Takeru myth. Later, under the reign of [[Emperor Tenmu]], the shrine was relocated to Seto, in [[Kurita District, Shiga|Kurita District]] and became the guardian shrine for Ōmi Province. However, in 755 the shrine was moved to its current location on orders of [[Empress Kōken]],Ponsonby-Fane, ''Visiting Shrines,'' p. 316. and [[Ōkuninushi]] was also installed as a deity at the shrine as a ''[[bunrei]]'' of [[Ōmiwa Shrine]]. The shrine appears in various [[Heian period]] documents, including the ''[[Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku]]'', ''[[Ruijū Kokushi]]'' and the ''[[Engishiki]]'', and came to be regarded as the ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of Ōmi Province.{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=978-4569669304 |language=Japanese}} When [[Minamoto no Yoritomo]] was sent to exile in [[Izu Province|Izu]] following the defeat of the [[Genji clan]] in the [[Heiji Rebellion]], he stopped to pray for the revival of the Genji at this shrine. In 1190, when the [[Heike clan]] was overthrown, he again prayed at this shrine in thanksgiving, and the shrine became a popular pilgrimage destination for good fortune.Ponsonby-Farne, ''Visiting Shrines,'' p. 321. The shrine was rebuilt in 1233 by [[Kujō Yoritsune]]. * '''1233''' (''[[Jōei]] 2''): [[Kujō Yoritsune]] completely rebuilt the structures of the shrine. In 1868, when [[Emperor Meiji]] made his first visit to [[Edo]], he dispatched an emissary to the shrine with a ''heikaku'' for the kami. In 1871, the shrine was listed under the name {{nihongo|'''Takebe Jinja'''|建部神社||}} as a prefectural shrine under the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines]] under [[State Shinto]]. In 1885, it was promoted to a {{nihongo|Imperial shrine, 2nd rank|官幣中社|[[Kanpei Chūsha]]}} and in 1900 to a {{nihongo|Imperial shrine, 1st rank|官幣大社|Kanpei-taisha}}.Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 124. In 1945, the shrine was used as the design for the first [[1000 yen note]] issued. In 1948 the shrine's name was officially changed to "Takebe Taisha".{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}} The shrine is 15 minutes on foot from [[Karahashimae Station]] on the [[Keihan Electric Railway]] [[Ishiyama Sakamoto Line]]. ==Gallery== Takebe {{Short description|Shinto shrine in Ōtsu, Shiga, Japan}} {{Use dmy dates|date=November 2019}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Takebe Taisha
建部大社 | image = Takebe-shrine.jpg | caption = ''Heiden'' of Takebe Taisha and three sacred sugi trees {{Infobox mapframe|tyep=shape|zoom=14|frame-width=240}} | map_type = Japan Shiga Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|34|58|24.6|N|135|54|48.62|E|type:landmark_region:JP|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = [[Yamato Takeru]]
[[Ōkuninushi|Ōkuninmushi no Mikoto]] | founded_by = [[Emperor Keiko]] | established = c.[[Kofun period]] | date_destroyed = | location = 16-1, Jinryō 1-chōme, Ōtsu, Shiga, Japan | website = {{Official|1=http://takebetaisha.jp/}} | architecture_style = ''[[Nagare-zukuri]]'' | festival = ''Reitaisai'' (15 April) | leadership = | footnotes ={{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=pink|text align=center|[[Important Cultural Properties of Japan|Important Cultural Property]]}} }} {{nihongo|'''Takebe Shrine'''|建部大社| Takebe Taisha}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in the city of [[Ōtsu, Shiga|Ōtsu]], [[Shiga Prefecture]], [[Japan]]. It is the ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of former [[Ōmi Province]].[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/images/uploads/EOS070712Ab.pdf "Nationwide List of ''Ichinomiya''," p. 1.]; retrieved 2011-08-010 The main ''[[kami]]'' enshrined are [[Ōkuninushi]] and [[Yamato Takeru]]. The shrine's main festival is held annually on April 15.{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=Shokoku jinja Ichinomiya Ninomiya San'nomiya |date=2015 |publisher= Yamakawa shuppansha |isbn=978-4634150867 |language=Japanese}} ==Enshrined ''kami''== The ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at Takebe Taisha are: * {{nihongo|'''[[Yamato Takeru]]'''| 日本武尊|}}, folk hero and the son of [[Emperor Keikō]] * {{nihongo|'''[[Ōkuninushi]]'''| 大己貴命|}}, the god of nation-building, agriculture, medicine, and protective magic ==History== According to the shrine's legend, its foundation has strong connections with the [[Yamato Takeru]] mythology. Following Yamato Takeru's death, his wife Princess Futaji resided at a place called Chigusatake, Takebe-go in what is now [[Kanzaki District, Shiga]], together with their son Takebe Inai Butsumei. A shrine was erected in that location 43rd year of the reign of the legendary [[Emperor Keikō]], who was Yamato Takeru's father. The place name "Takebe" is thought to come from the name "Takeru", and is also found in other places connected with theYamato Takeru myth. Later, under the reign of [[Emperor Tenmu]], the shrine was relocated to Seto, in [[Kurita District, Shiga|Kurita District]] and became the guardian shrine for Ōmi Province. However, in 755 the shrine was moved to its current location on orders of [[Empress Kōken]],Ponsonby-Fane, ''Visiting Shrines,'' p. 316. and [[Ōkuninushi]] was also installed as a deity at the shrine as a ''[[bunrei]]'' of [[Ōmiwa Shrine]]. The shrine appears in various [[Heian period]] documents, including the ''[[Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku]]'', ''[[Ruijū Kokushi]]'' and the ''[[Engishiki]]'', and came to be regarded as the ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of Ōmi Province.{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=978-4569669304 |language=Japanese}} When [[Minamoto no Yoritomo]] was sent to exile in [[Izu Province|Izu]] following the defeat of the [[Genji clan]] in the [[Heiji Rebellion]], he stopped to pray for the revival of the Genji at this shrine. In 1190, when the [[Heike clan]] was overthrown, he again prayed at this shrine in thanksgiving, and the shrine became a popular pilgrimage destination for good fortune.Ponsonby-Farne, ''Visiting Shrines,'' p. 321. The shrine was rebuilt in 1233 by [[Kujō Yoritsune]]. * '''1233''' (''[[Jōei]] 2''): [[Kujō Yoritsune]] completely rebuilt the structures of the shrine. In 1868, when [[Emperor Meiji]] made his first visit to [[Edo]], he dispatched an emissary to the shrine with a ''heikaku'' for the kami. In 1871, the shrine was listed under the name {{nihongo|'''Takebe Jinja'''|建部神社||}} as a prefectural shrine under the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines]] under [[State Shinto]]. In 1885, it was promoted to a {{nihongo|Imperial shrine, 2nd rank|官幣中社|[[Kanpei Chūsha]]}} and in 1900 to a {{nihongo|Imperial shrine, 1st rank|官幣大社|Kanpei-taisha}}.Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 124. In 1945, the shrine was used as the design for the first [[1000 yen note]] issued. In 1948 the shrine's name was officially changed to "Takebe Taisha".{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}} The shrine is 15 minutes on foot from [[Karahashimae Station]] on the [[Keihan Electric Railway]] [[Ishiyama Sakamoto Line]]. ==Gallery== Takebe Taisha1.jpg|First Torii Takebe Taisha honden.jpg|Honden Takebe Taisha homotsuden.jpg|Museum Takebe Taisha ishitoro.jpg|Stone lantern (ICP) Takebe Taisha2.jpg|Second Torii ==Cultural Properties== |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} *Wooden Statue of a Female deity (2 pcs), [[Heian period]].{{cite web |url= https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/3192|title=木造女神坐像|trans-title=Mokuzō nyoshinzazō|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} ==See The shrine's main festival is held annually on April 15.{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=Shokoku jinja Ichinomiya Ninomiya San'nomiya |date=2015 |publisher= Yamakawa shuppansha |isbn=978-4634150867 |language=Japanese}} ==Enshrined ''kami''== The ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at Takebe Taisha are: * {{nihongo|'''[[Yamato Takeru]]'''| 日本武尊|}}, folk hero and the son of [[Emperor Keikō]] * {{nihongo|'''[[Ōkuninushi]]'''| 大己貴命|}}, the god of nation-building, agriculture, medicine, and protective magic ==History== According to the shrine's legend, its foundation has strong connections with the [[Yamato Takeru]] mythology. Following Yamato Takeru's death, his wife Princess Futaji resided at a place called Chigusatake, Takebe-go in what is now [[Kanzaki District, Shiga]], together with their son Takebe Inai Butsumei. A shrine was erected in that location 43rd year of the reign of the legendary [[Emperor Keikō]], who was Yamato Takeru's father. The place name "Takebe" is thought to come from the name "Takeru", and is also found in other places connected with theYamato Takeru myth. Later, under the reign of [[Emperor Tenmu]], the shrine was relocated to Seto, in [[Kurita District, Shiga|Kurita District]] and became the guardian shrine for Ōmi Province. However, in 755 the shrine was moved to its current location on orders of [[Empress Kōken]],Ponsonby-Fane, ''Visiting Shrines,'' p. 316. and [[Ōkuninushi]] was also installed as a deity at the shrine as a ''[[bunrei]]'' of [[Ōmiwa Shrine]]. The shrine appears in various [[Heian period]] documents, including the ''[[Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku]]'', ''[[Ruijū Kokushi]]'' and the ''[[Engishiki]]'', and came to be regarded as the ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of Ōmi Province.{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=978-4569669304 |language=Japanese}} When [[Minamoto no Yoritomo]] was sent to exile in [[Izu Province|Izu]] following the defeat of the [[Genji clan]] in the [[Heiji Rebellion]], he stopped to pray for the revival of the Genji at this shrine. In 1190, when the [[Heike clan]] was overthrown, he again prayed at this shrine in thanksgiving, and the shrine became a popular pilgrimage destination for good fortune.Ponsonby-Farne, ''Visiting Shrines,'' p. 321. The shrine was rebuilt in 1233 by [[Kujō Yoritsune]]. * '''1233''' (''[[Jōei]] 2''): [[Kujō Yoritsune]] completely rebuilt the structures of the shrine. In 1868, when [[Emperor Meiji]] made his first visit to [[Edo]], he dispatched an emissary to the shrine with a ''heikaku'' for the kami. In 1871, the shrine was listed under the name {{nihongo|'''Takebe Jinja'''|建部神社||}} as a prefectural shrine under the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines]] under [[State Shinto]]. In 1885, it was promoted to a {{nihongo|Imperial shrine, 2nd rank|官幣中社|[[Kanpei Chūsha]]}} and in 1900 to a {{nihongo|Imperial shrine, 1st rank|官幣大社|Kanpei-taisha}}.Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 124. In 1945, the shrine was used as the design for the first [[1000 yen note]] issued. In 1948 the shrine's name was officially changed to "Takebe Taisha".{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}} The shrine is 15 minutes on foot from [[Karahashimae Station]] on the [[Keihan Electric Railway]] [[Ishiyama Sakamoto Line]]. ==Gallery== Takebe Taisha1.jpg|First Torii Takebe Taisha honden.jpg|Honden Takebe Taisha homotsuden.jpg|Museum Takebe Taisha ishitoro.jpg|Stone lantern (ICP) Takebe Taisha2.jpg|Second Torii ==Cultural Properties== |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} *Wooden Statue of a Female deity (2 pcs), [[Heian period]].{{cite web |url= https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/3192|title=木造女神坐像|trans-title=Mokuzō nyoshinzazō|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} ==See |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}} The shrine is 15 minutes on foot from [[Karahashimae Station]] on the [[Keihan Electric Railway]] [[Ishiyama Sakamoto Line]]. ==Gallery== Takebe {{Short description|Shinto shrine in Ōtsu, Shiga, Japan}} {{Use dmy dates|date=November 2019}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Takebe Taisha
建部大社 | image = Takebe-shrine.jpg | caption = ''Heiden'' of Takebe Taisha and three sacred sugi trees {{Infobox mapframe|tyep=shape|zoom=14|frame-width=240}} | map_type = Japan Shiga Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|34|58|24.6|N|135|54|48.62|E|type:landmark_region:JP|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = [[Yamato Takeru]]
[[Ōkuninushi|Ōkuninmushi no Mikoto]] | founded_by = [[Emperor Keiko]] | established = c.[[Kofun period]] | date_destroyed = | location = 16-1, Jinryō 1-chōme, Ōtsu, Shiga, Japan | website = {{Official|1=http://takebetaisha.jp/}} | architecture_style = ''[[Nagare-zukuri]]'' | festival = ''Reitaisai'' (15 April) | leadership = | footnotes ={{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=pink|text align=center|[[Important Cultural Properties of Japan|Important Cultural Property]]}} }} {{nihongo|'''Takebe Shrine'''|建部大社| Takebe Taisha}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in the city of [[Ōtsu, Shiga|Ōtsu]], [[Shiga Prefecture]], [[Japan]]. It is the ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of former [[Ōmi Province]].[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/images/uploads/EOS070712Ab.pdf "Nationwide List of ''Ichinomiya''," p. 1.]; retrieved 2011-08-010 The main ''[[kami]]'' enshrined are [[Ōkuninushi]] and [[Yamato Takeru]]. The shrine's main festival is held annually on April 15.{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=Shokoku jinja Ichinomiya Ninomiya San'nomiya |date=2015 |publisher= Yamakawa shuppansha |isbn=978-4634150867 |language=Japanese}} ==Enshrined ''kami''== The ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at Takebe Taisha are: * {{nihongo|'''[[Yamato Takeru]]'''| 日本武尊|}}, folk hero and the son of [[Emperor Keikō]] * {{nihongo|'''[[Ōkuninushi]]'''| 大己貴命|}}, the god of nation-building, agriculture, medicine, and protective magic ==History== According to the shrine's legend, its foundation has strong connections with the [[Yamato Takeru]] mythology. Following Yamato Takeru's death, his wife Princess Futaji resided at a place called Chigusatake, Takebe-go in what is now [[Kanzaki District, Shiga]], together with their son Takebe Inai Butsumei. A shrine was erected in that location 43rd year of the reign of the legendary [[Emperor Keikō]], who was Yamato Takeru's father. The place name "Takebe" is thought to come from the name "Takeru", and is also found in other places connected with theYamato Takeru myth. Later, under the reign of [[Emperor Tenmu]], the shrine was relocated to Seto, in [[Kurita District, Shiga|Kurita District]] and became the guardian shrine for Ōmi Province. However, in 755 the shrine was moved to its current location on orders of [[Empress Kōken]],Ponsonby-Fane, ''Visiting Shrines,'' p. 316. and [[Ōkuninushi]] was also installed as a deity at the shrine as a ''[[bunrei]]'' of [[Ōmiwa Shrine]]. The shrine appears in various [[Heian period]] documents, including the ''[[Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku]]'', ''[[Ruijū Kokushi]]'' and the ''[[Engishiki]]'', and came to be regarded as the ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of Ōmi Province.{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=978-4569669304 |language=Japanese}} When [[Minamoto no Yoritomo]] was sent to exile in [[Izu Province|Izu]] following the defeat of the [[Genji clan]] in the [[Heiji Rebellion]], he stopped to pray for the revival of the Genji at this shrine. In 1190, when the [[Heike clan]] was overthrown, he again prayed at this shrine in thanksgiving, and the shrine became a popular pilgrimage destination for good fortune.Ponsonby-Farne, ''Visiting Shrines,'' p. 321. The shrine was rebuilt in 1233 by [[Kujō Yoritsune]]. * '''1233''' (''[[Jōei]] 2''): [[Kujō Yoritsune]] completely rebuilt the structures of the shrine. In 1868, when [[Emperor Meiji]] made his first visit to [[Edo]], he dispatched an emissary to the shrine with a ''heikaku'' for the kami. In 1871, the shrine was listed under the name {{nihongo|'''Takebe Jinja'''|建部神社||}} as a prefectural shrine under the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines]] under [[State Shinto]]. In 1885, it was promoted to a {{nihongo|Imperial shrine, 2nd rank|官幣中社|[[Kanpei Chūsha]]}} and in 1900 to a {{nihongo|Imperial shrine, 1st rank|官幣大社|Kanpei-taisha}}.Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 124. In 1945, the shrine was used as the design for the first [[1000 yen note]] issued. In 1948 the shrine's name was officially changed to "Takebe Taisha".{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}} The shrine is 15 minutes on foot from [[Karahashimae Station]] on the [[Keihan Electric Railway]] [[Ishiyama Sakamoto Line]]. ==Gallery== Takebe Taisha1.jpg|First Torii Takebe Taisha honden.jpg|Honden Takebe Taisha homotsuden.jpg|Museum Takebe Taisha ishitoro.jpg|Stone lantern (ICP) Takebe Taisha2.jpg|Second Torii ==Cultural Properties== ===Important Cultural Properties=== *[[Tōrō]] stone lantern, dated 1270.{{cite web |url= https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/6834|title=石燈籠|trans-title=Ishi Tōrō|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} *Wooden Statue of a Female deity (2 pcs), [[Heian period]].{{cite web |url= https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/3192|title=木造女神坐像|trans-title=Mokuzō nyoshinzazō|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} ==See also== *[[List of Shinto shrines]] *[[Ichinomiya]] ==References== {{reflist}} ==External links== {{commons Category|Takebe Taisha}} *[http://takebetaisha.jp/ Official website] 建部大社 | image = Takebe-shrine.jpg | caption = ''Heiden'' of Takebe Taisha and three sacred sugi trees {{Infobox mapframe|tyep=shape|zoom=14|frame-width=240}} | map_type = Japan Shiga Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|34|58|24.6|N|135|54|48.62|E|type:landmark_region:JP|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = [[Yamato Takeru]]
[[Ōkuninushi|Ōkuninmushi no Mikoto]] | founded_by = [[Emperor Keiko]] | established = c.[[Kofun period]] | date_destroyed = | location = 16-1, Jinryō 1-chōme, Ōtsu, Shiga, Japan | website = {{Official|1=http://takebetaisha.jp/}} | architecture_style = ''[[Nagare-zukuri]]'' | festival = ''Reitaisai'' (15 April) | leadership = | footnotes ={{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=pink|text align=center|[[Important Cultural Properties of Japan|Important Cultural Property]]}} }} {{nihongo|'''Takebe Shrine'''|建部大社| Takebe Taisha}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in the city of [[Ōtsu, Shiga|Ōtsu]], [[Shiga Prefecture]], [[Japan]]. It is the ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of former [[Ōmi Province]].[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/images/uploads/EOS070712Ab.pdf "Nationwide List of ''Ichinomiya''," p. 1.]; retrieved 2011-08-010 The main ''[[kami]]'' enshrined are [[Ōkuninushi]] and [[Yamato Takeru]]. The shrine's main festival is held annually on April 15.{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=Shokoku jinja Ichinomiya Ninomiya San'nomiya |date=2015 |publisher= Yamakawa shuppansha |isbn=978-4634150867 |language=Japanese}} ==Enshrined ''kami''== The ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at Takebe Taisha are: * {{nihongo|'''[[Yamato Takeru]]'''| 日本武尊|}}, folk hero and the son of [[Emperor Keikō]] * {{nihongo|'''[[Ōkuninushi]]'''| 大己貴命|}}, the god of nation-building, agriculture, medicine, and protective magic ==History== According to the shrine's legend, its foundation has strong connections with the [[Yamato Takeru]] mythology. Following Yamato Takeru's death, his wife Princess Futaji resided at a place called Chigusatake, Takebe-go in what is now [[Kanzaki District, Shiga]], together with their son Takebe Inai Butsumei. A shrine was erected in that location 43rd year of the reign of the legendary [[Emperor Keikō]], who was Yamato Takeru's father. The place name "Takebe" is thought to come from the name "Takeru", and is also found in other places connected with theYamato Takeru myth. Later, under the reign of [[Emperor Tenmu]], the shrine was relocated to Seto, in [[Kurita District, Shiga|Kurita District]] and became the guardian shrine for Ōmi Province. However, in 755 the shrine was moved to its current location on orders of [[Empress Kōken]],Ponsonby-Fane, ''Visiting Shrines,'' p. 316. and [[Ōkuninushi]] was also installed as a deity at the shrine as a ''[[bunrei]]'' of [[Ōmiwa Shrine]]. The shrine appears in various [[Heian period]] documents, including the ''[[Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku]]'', ''[[Ruijū Kokushi]]'' and the ''[[Engishiki]]'', and came to be regarded as the ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of Ōmi Province.{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=978-4569669304 |language=Japanese}} When [[Minamoto no Yoritomo]] was sent to exile in [[Izu Province|Izu]] following the defeat of the [[Genji clan]] in the [[Heiji Rebellion]], he stopped to pray for the revival of the Genji at this shrine. In 1190, when the [[Heike clan]] was overthrown, he again prayed at this shrine in thanksgiving, and the shrine became a popular pilgrimage destination for good fortune.Ponsonby-Farne, ''Visiting Shrines,'' p. 321. The shrine was rebuilt in 1233 by [[Kujō Yoritsune]]. * '''1233''' (''[[Jōei]] 2''): [[Kujō Yoritsune]] completely rebuilt the structures of the shrine. In 1868, when [[Emperor Meiji]] made his first visit to [[Edo]], he dispatched an emissary to the shrine with a ''heikaku'' for the kami. In 1871, the shrine was listed under the name {{nihongo|'''Takebe Jinja'''|建部神社||}} as a prefectural shrine under the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines]] under [[State Shinto]]. In 1885, it was promoted to a {{nihongo|Imperial shrine, 2nd rank|官幣中社|[[Kanpei Chūsha]]}} and in 1900 to a {{nihongo|Imperial shrine, 1st rank|官幣大社|Kanpei-taisha}}.Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 124. In 1945, the shrine was used as the design for the first [[1000 yen note]] issued. In 1948 the shrine's name was officially changed to "Takebe Taisha".{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}} The shrine is 15 minutes on foot from [[Karahashimae Station]] on the [[Keihan Electric Railway]] [[Ishiyama Sakamoto Line]]. ==Gallery== Takebe Taisha1.jpg|First Torii Takebe Taisha honden.jpg|Honden Takebe Taisha homotsuden.jpg|Museum Takebe Taisha ishitoro.jpg|Stone lantern (ICP) Takebe Taisha2.jpg|Second Torii ==Cultural Properties== ===Important Cultural Properties=== *[[Tōrō]] stone lantern, dated 1270.{{cite web |url= https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/6834|title=石燈籠|trans-title=Ishi Tōrō|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} *Wooden Statue of a Female deity (2 pcs), [[Heian period]].{{cite web |url= https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/3192|title=木造女神坐像|trans-title=Mokuzō nyoshinzazō|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} ==See also== *[[List of Shinto shrines]] *[[Ichinomiya]] ==References== {{reflist}} ==External links== {{commons Category|Takebe Taisha}} *[http://takebetaisha.jp/ Official website] The shrine appears in various [[Heian period]] documents, including the ''[[Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku]]'', ''[[Ruijū Kokushi]]'' and the ''[[Engishiki]]'', and came to be regarded as the ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of Ōmi Province.{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=978-4569669304 |language=Japanese}} When [[Minamoto no Yoritomo]] was sent to exile in [[Izu Province|Izu]] following the defeat of the [[Genji clan]] in the [[Heiji Rebellion]], he stopped to pray for the revival of the Genji at this shrine. In 1190, when the [[Heike clan]] was overthrown, he again prayed at this shrine in thanksgiving, and the shrine became a popular pilgrimage destination for good fortune.Ponsonby-Farne, ''Visiting Shrines,'' p. 321. The shrine was rebuilt in 1233 by [[Kujō Yoritsune]]. * '''1233''' (''[[Jōei]] 2''): [[Kujō Yoritsune]] completely rebuilt the structures of the shrine. In 1868, when [[Emperor Meiji]] made his first visit to [[Edo]], he dispatched an emissary to the shrine with a ''heikaku'' for the kami. In 1871, the shrine was listed under the name {{nihongo|'''Takebe Jinja'''|建部神社||}} as a prefectural shrine under the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines]] under [[State Shinto]]. In 1885, it was promoted to a {{nihongo|Imperial shrine, 2nd rank|官幣中社|[[Kanpei Chūsha]]}} and in 1900 to a {{nihongo|Imperial shrine, 1st rank|官幣大社|Kanpei-taisha}}.Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 124. In 1945, the shrine was used as the design for the first [[1000 yen note]] issued. In 1948 the shrine's name was officially changed to "Takebe Taisha".{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}} The shrine is 15 minutes on foot from [[Karahashimae Station]] on the [[Keihan Electric Railway]] [[Ishiyama Sakamoto Line]]. ==Gallery== Takebe Taisha1.jpg|First Torii Takebe Taisha honden.jpg|Honden Takebe Taisha homotsuden.jpg|Museum Takebe Taisha ishitoro.jpg|Stone lantern (ICP) Takebe Taisha2.jpg|Second Torii ==Cultural Properties== {{Short description|Shinto shrine in Ōtsu, Shiga, Japan}} {{Use dmy dates|date=November 2019}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Takebe Taisha
建部大社 | image = Takebe-shrine.jpg | caption = ''Heiden'' of Takebe Taisha and three sacred sugi trees {{Infobox mapframe|tyep=shape|zoom=14|frame-width=240}} | map_type = Japan Shiga Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|34|58|24.6|N|135|54|48.62|E|type:landmark_region:JP|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = [[Yamato Takeru]]
[[Ōkuninushi|Ōkuninmushi no Mikoto]] | founded_by = [[Emperor Keiko]] | established = c.[[Kofun period]] | date_destroyed = | location = 16-1, Jinryō 1-chōme, Ōtsu, Shiga, Japan | website = {{Official|1=http://takebetaisha.jp/}} | architecture_style = ''[[Nagare-zukuri]]'' | festival = ''Reitaisai'' (15 April) | leadership = | footnotes ={{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=pink|text align=center|[[Important Cultural Properties of Japan|Important Cultural Property]]}} }} {{nihongo|'''Takebe Shrine'''|建部大社| Takebe Taisha}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in the city of [[Ōtsu, Shiga|Ōtsu]], [[Shiga Prefecture]], [[Japan]]. It is the ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of former [[Ōmi Province]].[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/images/uploads/EOS070712Ab.pdf "Nationwide List of ''Ichinomiya''," p. 1.]; retrieved 2011-08-010 The main ''[[kami]]'' enshrined are [[Ōkuninushi]] and [[Yamato Takeru]]. The shrine's main festival is held annually on April 15.{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=Shokoku jinja Ichinomiya Ninomiya San'nomiya |date=2015 |publisher= Yamakawa shuppansha |isbn=978-4634150867 |language=Japanese}} ==Enshrined ''kami''== The ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at Takebe Taisha are: * {{nihongo|'''[[Yamato Takeru]]'''| 日本武尊|}}, folk hero and the son of [[Emperor Keikō]] * {{nihongo|'''[[Ōkuninushi]]'''| 大己貴命|}}, the god of nation-building, agriculture, medicine, and protective magic ==History== According to the shrine's legend, its foundation has strong connections with the [[Yamato Takeru]] mythology. Following Yamato Takeru's death, his wife Princess Futaji resided at a place called Chigusatake, Takebe-go in what is now [[Kanzaki District, Shiga]], together with their son Takebe Inai Butsumei. A shrine was erected in that location 43rd year of the reign of the legendary [[Emperor Keikō]], who was Yamato Takeru's father. The place name "Takebe" is thought to come from the name "Takeru", and is also found in other places connected with theYamato Takeru myth. Later, under the reign of [[Emperor Tenmu]], the shrine was relocated to Seto, in [[Kurita District, Shiga|Kurita District]] and became the guardian shrine for Ōmi Province. However, in 755 the shrine was moved to its current location on orders of [[Empress Kōken]],Ponsonby-Fane, ''Visiting Shrines,'' p. 316. and [[Ōkuninushi]] was also installed as a deity at the shrine as a ''[[bunrei]]'' of [[Ōmiwa Shrine]]. The shrine appears in various [[Heian period]] documents, including the ''[[Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku]]'', ''[[Ruijū Kokushi]]'' and the ''[[Engishiki]]'', and came to be regarded as the ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of Ōmi Province.{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=978-4569669304 |language=Japanese}} When [[Minamoto no Yoritomo]] was sent to exile in [[Izu Province|Izu]] following the defeat of the [[Genji clan]] in the [[Heiji Rebellion]], he stopped to pray for the revival of the Genji at this shrine. In 1190, when the [[Heike clan]] was overthrown, he again prayed at this shrine in thanksgiving, and the shrine became a popular pilgrimage destination for good fortune.Ponsonby-Farne, ''Visiting Shrines,'' p. 321. The shrine was rebuilt in 1233 by [[Kujō Yoritsune]]. * '''1233''' (''[[Jōei]] 2''): [[Kujō Yoritsune]] completely rebuilt the structures of the shrine. In 1868, when [[Emperor Meiji]] made his first visit to [[Edo]], he dispatched an emissary to the shrine with a ''heikaku'' for the kami. In 1871, the shrine was listed under the name {{nihongo|'''Takebe Jinja'''|建部神社||}} as a prefectural shrine under the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines]] under [[State Shinto]]. In 1885, it was promoted to a {{nihongo|Imperial shrine, 2nd rank|官幣中社|[[Kanpei Chūsha]]}} and in 1900 to a {{nihongo|Imperial shrine, 1st rank|官幣大社|Kanpei-taisha}}.Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 124. In 1945, the shrine was used as the design for the first [[1000 yen note]] issued. In 1948 the shrine's name was officially changed to "Takebe Taisha".{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}} The shrine is 15 minutes on foot from [[Karahashimae Station]] on the [[Keihan Electric Railway]] [[Ishiyama Sakamoto Line]]. ==Gallery== Takebe Taisha1.jpg|First Torii Takebe Taisha honden.jpg|Honden Takebe Taisha homotsuden.jpg|Museum Takebe Taisha ishitoro.jpg|Stone lantern (ICP) Takebe Taisha2.jpg|Second Torii ==Cultural Properties== ===Important Cultural Properties=== *[[Tōrō]] stone lantern, dated 1270.{{cite web |url= https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/6834|title=石燈籠|trans-title=Ishi Tōrō|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} *Wooden Statue of a Female deity (2 pcs), [[Heian period]].{{cite web |url= https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/3192|title=木造女神坐像|trans-title=Mokuzō nyoshinzazō|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} ==See also== *[[List of Shinto shrines]] *[[Ichinomiya]] ==References== {{reflist}} ==External links== {{commons Category|Takebe Taisha}} *[http://takebetaisha.jp/ Official website] *[https://otsu.or.jp/en/thingstodo/spot49 Biwako-Otsu Travel Guide] {{Shinto shrine}} [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:beppyo shrines without templates]] [[Category:Kanpei Taisha]] [[en:Takebe taisha]][[Category:Shinto shrines in Shiga Prefecture]] [[Category:Ichinomiya]] [[Category:Ōmi Province]] [[Category:Ōtsu]] [[Category:Myōjin Taisha]] [[Category:Taisha]] [[Category:Yamato Takeru Legend]] [[Category:Nagare-zukuri]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 1 6]][[en:Takebe taisha]]]] [[Category:mm]] [[en:Takebe taisha]][[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 1 6]][[en:Takebe taisha]]]] [[Category:mm]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 1 6]][[en:Takebe taisha]]]] [[Category:mm]] [[Category:dd]][[Category:Takebe taisha]][[Category:dd]] https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/3192|title=木造女神坐像|trans-title=Mokuzō nyoshinzazō|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} ==See {{Short description|Shinto shrine in Ōtsu, Shiga, Japan}} {{Use dmy dates|date=November 2019}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Takebe Taisha
建部大社 | image = Takebe-shrine.jpg | caption = ''Heiden'' of Takebe Taisha and three sacred sugi trees {{Infobox mapframe|tyep=shape|zoom=14|frame-width=240}} | map_type = Japan Shiga Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|34|58|24.6|N|135|54|48.62|E|type:landmark_region:JP|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = [[Yamato Takeru]]
[[Ōkuninushi|Ōkuninmushi no Mikoto]] | founded_by = [[Emperor Keiko]] | established = c.[[Kofun period]] | date_destroyed = | location = 16-1, Jinryō 1-chōme, Ōtsu, Shiga, Japan | website = {{Official|1=http://takebetaisha.jp/}} | architecture_style = ''[[Nagare-zukuri]]'' | festival = ''Reitaisai'' (15 April) | leadership = | footnotes ={{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=pink|text align=center|[[Important Cultural Properties of Japan|Important Cultural Property]]}} }} {{nihongo|'''Takebe Shrine'''|建部大社| Takebe Taisha}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in the city of [[Ōtsu, Shiga|Ōtsu]], [[Shiga Prefecture]], [[Japan]]. It is the ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of former [[Ōmi Province]].[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/images/uploads/EOS070712Ab.pdf "Nationwide List of ''Ichinomiya''," p. 1.]; retrieved 2011-08-010 The main ''[[kami]]'' enshrined are [[Ōkuninushi]] and [[Yamato Takeru]]. The shrine's main festival is held annually on April 15.{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=Shokoku jinja Ichinomiya Ninomiya San'nomiya |date=2015 |publisher= Yamakawa shuppansha |isbn=978-4634150867 |language=Japanese}} ==Enshrined ''kami''== The ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at Takebe Taisha are: * {{nihongo|'''[[Yamato Takeru]]'''| 日本武尊|}}, folk hero and the son of [[Emperor Keikō]] * {{nihongo|'''[[Ōkuninushi]]'''| 大己貴命|}}, the god of nation-building, agriculture, medicine, and protective magic ==History== According to the shrine's legend, its foundation has strong connections with the [[Yamato Takeru]] mythology. Following Yamato Takeru's death, his wife Princess Futaji resided at a place called Chigusatake, Takebe-go in what is now [[Kanzaki District, Shiga]], together with their son Takebe Inai Butsumei. A shrine was erected in that location 43rd year of the reign of the legendary [[Emperor Keikō]], who was Yamato Takeru's father. The place name "Takebe" is thought to come from the name "Takeru", and is also found in other places connected with theYamato Takeru myth. Later, under the reign of [[Emperor Tenmu]], the shrine was relocated to Seto, in [[Kurita District, Shiga|Kurita District]] and became the guardian shrine for Ōmi Province. However, in 755 the shrine was moved to its current location on orders of [[Empress Kōken]],Ponsonby-Fane, ''Visiting Shrines,'' p. 316. and [[Ōkuninushi]] was also installed as a deity at the shrine as a ''[[bunrei]]'' of [[Ōmiwa Shrine]]. The shrine appears in various [[Heian period]] documents, including the ''[[Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku]]'', ''[[Ruijū Kokushi]]'' and the ''[[Engishiki]]'', and came to be regarded as the ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of Ōmi Province.{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=978-4569669304 |language=Japanese}} When [[Minamoto no Yoritomo]] was sent to exile in [[Izu Province|Izu]] following the defeat of the [[Genji clan]] in the [[Heiji Rebellion]], he stopped to pray for the revival of the Genji at this shrine. In 1190, when the [[Heike clan]] was overthrown, he again prayed at this shrine in thanksgiving, and the shrine became a popular pilgrimage destination for good fortune.Ponsonby-Farne, ''Visiting Shrines,'' p. 321. The shrine was rebuilt in 1233 by [[Kujō Yoritsune]]. * '''1233''' (''[[Jōei]] 2''): [[Kujō Yoritsune]] completely rebuilt the structures of the shrine. In 1868, when [[Emperor Meiji]] made his first visit to [[Edo]], he dispatched an emissary to the shrine with a ''heikaku'' for the kami. In 1871, the shrine was listed under the name {{nihongo|'''Takebe Jinja'''|建部神社||}} as a prefectural shrine under the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines]] under [[State Shinto]]. In 1885, it was promoted to a {{nihongo|Imperial shrine, 2nd rank|官幣中社|[[Kanpei Chūsha]]}} and in 1900 to a {{nihongo|Imperial shrine, 1st rank|官幣大社|Kanpei-taisha}}.Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 124. In 1945, the shrine was used as the design for the first [[1000 yen note]] issued. In 1948 the shrine's name was officially changed to "Takebe Taisha".{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}} The shrine is 15 minutes on foot from [[Karahashimae Station]] on the [[Keihan Electric Railway]] [[Ishiyama Sakamoto Line]]. ==Gallery== Takebe Taisha1.jpg|First Torii Takebe Taisha honden.jpg|Honden Takebe Taisha homotsuden.jpg|Museum Takebe Taisha ishitoro.jpg|Stone lantern (ICP) Takebe Taisha2.jpg|Second Torii ==Cultural Properties== ===Important Cultural Properties=== *[[Tōrō]] stone lantern, dated 1270.{{cite web |url= https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/6834|title=石燈籠|trans-title=Ishi Tōrō|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} *Wooden Statue of a Female deity (2 pcs), [[Heian period]].{{cite web |url= https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/3192|title=木造女神坐像|trans-title=Mokuzō nyoshinzazō|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} ==See also== *[[List of Shinto shrines]] *[[Ichinomiya]] ==References== {{reflist}} ==External links== {{commons Category|Takebe Taisha}} *[http://takebetaisha.jp/ Official website] taisha]][[Category:Shinto shrines in Shiga Prefecture]] [[Category:Ichinomiya]] [[Category:Ōmi Province]] [[Category:Ōtsu]] [[Category:Myōjin Taisha]] [[Category:Taisha]] [[Category:Yamato Takeru {{Short description|Shinto shrine in Ōtsu, Shiga, Japan}} {{Use dmy dates|date=November 2019}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Takebe Taisha
建部大社 | image = Takebe-shrine.jpg | caption = ''Heiden'' of Takebe Taisha and three sacred sugi trees {{Infobox mapframe|tyep=shape|zoom=14|frame-width=240}} | map_type = Japan Shiga Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|34|58|24.6|N|135|54|48.62|E|type:landmark_region:JP|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = 1 | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = [[Yamato Takeru]]
[[Ōkuninushi|Ōkuninmushi no Mikoto]] | founded_by = [[Emperor Keiko]] | established = c.[[Kofun period]] | date_destroyed = | location = 16-1, Jinryō 1-chōme, Ōtsu, Shiga, Japan | website = {{Official|1=http://takebetaisha.jp/}} | architecture_style = ''[[Nagare-zukuri]]'' | festival = ''Reitaisai'' (15 April) | leadership = | footnotes ={{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=pink|text align=center|[[Important Cultural Properties of Japan|Important Cultural Property]]}} }} {{nihongo|'''Takebe Shrine'''|建部大社| Takebe Taisha}} is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in the city of [[Ōtsu, Shiga|Ōtsu]], [[Shiga Prefecture]], [[Japan]]. It is the ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of former [[Ōmi Province]].[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/images/uploads/EOS070712Ab.pdf "Nationwide List of ''Ichinomiya''," p. 1.]; retrieved 2011-08-010 The main ''[[kami]]'' enshrined are [[Ōkuninushi]] and [[Yamato Takeru]]. The shrine's main festival is held annually on April 15.{{cite book |last1=Shibuya |first1=Nobuhiro |title=Shokoku jinja Ichinomiya Ninomiya San'nomiya |date=2015 |publisher= Yamakawa shuppansha |isbn=978-4634150867 |language=Japanese}} ==Enshrined ''kami''== The ''[[kami]]'' enshrined at Takebe Taisha are: * {{nihongo|'''[[Yamato Takeru]]'''| 日本武尊|}}, folk hero and the son of [[Emperor Keikō]] * {{nihongo|'''[[Ōkuninushi]]'''| 大己貴命|}}, the god of nation-building, agriculture, medicine, and protective magic ==History== According to the shrine's legend, its foundation has strong connections with the [[Yamato Takeru]] mythology. Following Yamato Takeru's death, his wife Princess Futaji resided at a place called Chigusatake, Takebe-go in what is now [[Kanzaki District, Shiga]], together with their son Takebe Inai Butsumei. A shrine was erected in that location 43rd year of the reign of the legendary [[Emperor Keikō]], who was Yamato Takeru's father. The place name "Takebe" is thought to come from the name "Takeru", and is also found in other places connected with theYamato Takeru myth. Later, under the reign of [[Emperor Tenmu]], the shrine was relocated to Seto, in [[Kurita District, Shiga|Kurita District]] and became the guardian shrine for Ōmi Province. However, in 755 the shrine was moved to its current location on orders of [[Empress Kōken]],Ponsonby-Fane, ''Visiting Shrines,'' p. 316. and [[Ōkuninushi]] was also installed as a deity at the shrine as a ''[[bunrei]]'' of [[Ōmiwa Shrine]]. The shrine appears in various [[Heian period]] documents, including the ''[[Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku]]'', ''[[Ruijū Kokushi]]'' and the ''[[Engishiki]]'', and came to be regarded as the ''[[ichinomiya]]'' of Ōmi Province.{{cite book |last1=Yoshiki |first1=Emi |title=Zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' tettei gaido |date=2007 |publisher=PHP Institute |isbn=978-4569669304 |language=Japanese}} When [[Minamoto no Yoritomo]] was sent to exile in [[Izu Province|Izu]] following the defeat of the [[Genji clan]] in the [[Heiji Rebellion]], he stopped to pray for the revival of the Genji at this shrine. In 1190, when the [[Heike clan]] was overthrown, he again prayed at this shrine in thanksgiving, and the shrine became a popular pilgrimage destination for good fortune.Ponsonby-Farne, ''Visiting Shrines,'' p. 321. The shrine was rebuilt in 1233 by [[Kujō Yoritsune]]. * '''1233''' (''[[Jōei]] 2''): [[Kujō Yoritsune]] completely rebuilt the structures of the shrine. In 1868, when [[Emperor Meiji]] made his first visit to [[Edo]], he dispatched an emissary to the shrine with a ''heikaku'' for the kami. In 1871, the shrine was listed under the name {{nihongo|'''Takebe Jinja'''|建部神社||}} as a prefectural shrine under the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines]] under [[State Shinto]]. In 1885, it was promoted to a {{nihongo|Imperial shrine, 2nd rank|官幣中社|[[Kanpei Chūsha]]}} and in 1900 to a {{nihongo|Imperial shrine, 1st rank|官幣大社|Kanpei-taisha}}.Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 124. In 1945, the shrine was used as the design for the first [[1000 yen note]] issued. In 1948 the shrine's name was officially changed to "Takebe Taisha".{{cite book |last1=Okada |first1=Shoji |title=Taiyō no chizuchō 24 zenkoku 'Ichinomiya' meguri |date=2014 |publisher=Heibonsha |isbn=978-4582945614 |language=Japanese}} The shrine is 15 minutes on foot from [[Karahashimae Station]] on the [[Keihan Electric Railway]] [[Ishiyama Sakamoto Line]]. ==Gallery== Takebe Taisha1.jpg|First Torii Takebe Taisha honden.jpg|Honden Takebe Taisha homotsuden.jpg|Museum Takebe Taisha ishitoro.jpg|Stone lantern (ICP) Takebe Taisha2.jpg|Second Torii ==Cultural Properties== ===Important Cultural Properties=== *[[Tōrō]] stone lantern, dated 1270.{{cite web |url= https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/6834|title=石燈籠|trans-title=Ishi Tōrō|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} *Wooden Statue of a Female deity (2 pcs), [[Heian period]].{{cite web |url= https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/3192|title=木造女神坐像|trans-title=Mokuzō nyoshinzazō|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2020}} ==See also== *[[List of Shinto shrines]] *[[Ichinomiya]] ==References== {{reflist}} ==External links== {{commons Category|Takebe Taisha}} *[http://takebetaisha.jp/ Official website] taisha]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:bump February 13, 2025 bump]][[en:Takebe taisha]][[Category:bump February 13, 2025 bump]] [[en:Takebe taisha]][[en:Takebe taisha]] {{Beppyo Shrines}}
